Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-bounded
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-bounded]
{Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a Bounded Domain}}{%
  Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a Bounded Domain}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-bounded] {Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a Bounded Domain}{% Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a Bounded Domain}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Which algebraic operations preserve uniform continuity on
  a \emph{bounded} domain?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Which algebraic operations preserve uniform continuity on a bounded domain?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
All of: $f+g$, $f-g$, $cf$, and $fg$. Also $f/g$ if $g$
  is bounded away from zero. Boundedness of the domain forces $f$
  and $g$ to be bounded, which controls the cross-terms in the
  product estimate.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: All of: f+g, f-g, cf, and fg. Also f/g if g is bounded away from zero. Boundedness of the domain forces f and g to be bounded, which controls the cross-terms in the product estimate.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Key step: UC on bounded set $\Rightarrow$ bounded image.
  Then $|(fg)(x)-(fg)(u)| \leq M_f|g(x)-g(u)| + M_g|f(x)-f(u)|$
  and both terms are controlled.

Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-general
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-general]
{Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a General Domain}}{%
  Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a General Domain}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:algebra-of-uniform-continuity-general] {Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a General Domain}{% Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a General Domain}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Which algebraic operations preserve uniform continuity on
  a general domain?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Which algebraic operations preserve uniform continuity on a general domain?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f+g$, $f-g$, and $cf$ are UC whenever $f$ and $g$ are.
  Products fail in general: $x \cdot x = x^2$ is not UC on
  $\mathbb{R}$ even though $x$ is.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: f+g, f-g, and cf are UC whenever f and g are. Products fail in general: x x = x^2 is not UC on ℝ even though x is.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
$\delta = \min(\delta_f, \delta_g)$ works for sums and
  differences. Products need a bound on $f$ and $g$ to control the
  cross-terms.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:bilipschitz-inverse-is-lipschitz
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:bilipschitz-inverse-is-lipschitz]
{Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz}}{%
  Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:bilipschitz-inverse-is-lipschitz] {Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz}{% Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
If $f$ is bi-Lipschitz with bounds $\alpha$ and $K$, what
  can be said about $f^{-1}$?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: If f is bi-Lipschitz with bounds and K, what can be said about f^{-1}?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f^{-1}$ exists and is bi-Lipschitz with bounds $1/K$
  and $1/\alpha$. In particular $f^{-1}$ is Lipschitz with
  constant $1/\alpha$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: f^{-1} exists and is bi-Lipschitz with bounds 1/K and 1/ . In particular f^{-1} is Lipschitz with constant 1/ .
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Substitute $y=f(x)$, $v=f(u)$ and divide the lower
  Lipschitz bound of $f$ by $\alpha$.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:bilipschitz-inverse-is-lipschitz
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:bilipschitz-inverse-is-lipschitz]
{Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz}}{%
  Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:bilipschitz-inverse-is-lipschitz] {Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz}{% Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Sketch the proof of: \texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:bilipschitz-inverse-is-lipschitz]
{Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz}}{%
  Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz}.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: % \hyperref[prf:bilipschitz-inverse-is-lipschitz] {Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz}{% Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
%
  1. Injectivity: lower bound forces $x \neq u \Rightarrow f(x) \neq f(u)$. \\
  2. Set $y = f(x)$, $v = f(u)$. Lower bound on $f$:
     $\alpha|f^{-1}(y)-f^{-1}(v)| \leq |y-v|$;
     divide by $\alpha$ to get Lipschitz constant $1/\alpha$ for $f^{-1}$. \\
  3. Upper bound on $f$: $|y-v| \leq K|f^{-1}(y)-f^{-1}(v)|$;
     divide by $K$ to get lower bound $1/K$ for $f^{-1}$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: % 1. Injectivity: lower bound forces x ≠ u ⇒ f(x) ≠ f(u). \\ 2. Set y = f(x), v = f(u). Lower bound on f: |f^{-1}(y)-f^{-1}(v)| ≤ |y-v|; divide by to get Lipschitz constant 1/ for f^{-1}. \\ 3. Upper bound on f: |y-v| ≤ K|f^{-1}(y)-f^{-1}(v)|; divide by K to get lower bound 1/K for f^{-1}.

Analysis · Continuity.label: prop:uniform-continuity-cauchy
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:cauchy-sequences-preserved]
{Uniform Continuity Preserves Cauchy Sequences}}{%
  Uniform Continuity Preserves Cauchy Sequences}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:cauchy-sequences-preserved] {Uniform Continuity Preserves Cauchy Sequences}{% Uniform Continuity Preserves Cauchy Sequences}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: What does uniform continuity do to Cauchy sequences?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What does uniform continuity do to Cauchy sequences?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: Uniform continuity maps Cauchy sequences to Cauchy sequences.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: Uniform continuity maps Cauchy sequences to Cauchy sequences.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
One $\varepsilon$ drives both steps: uniform continuity
  converts $\varepsilon$ to $\delta$; the Cauchy condition converts
  $\delta$ to $N$.

Analysis · Continuity.label: prop:uniform-continuity-cauchy
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:cauchy-sequences-preserved]
{Uniform Continuity Preserves Cauchy Sequences}}{%
  Uniform Continuity Preserves Cauchy Sequences}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:cauchy-sequences-preserved] {Uniform Continuity Preserves Cauchy Sequences}{% Uniform Continuity Preserves Cauchy Sequences}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
$f$ uniformly continuous on $S$,\; $(x_n) \subseteq S$ Cauchy
$\;\Rightarrow\;$ $(f(x_n))$ Cauchy.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: f uniformly continuous on S,\; (x_n) ⊆ S Cauchy \;⇒\; (f(x_n)) Cauchy.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: This is the Proposition: % \hyperref[prf:cauchy-sequences-preserved] {Uniform Continuity Preserves Cauchy Sequences}{% Uniform Continuity Preserves Cauchy Sequences}

Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:composition-of-continuous-functions]
{Composition of Continuous Functions}}{%
  Composition of Continuous Functions}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:composition-of-continuous-functions] {Composition of Continuous Functions}{% Composition of Continuous Functions}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Sketch the proof of: \texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:composition-of-continuous-functions]
{Composition of Continuous Functions}}{%
  Composition of Continuous Functions}.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: % \hyperref[prf:composition-of-continuous-functions] {Composition of Continuous Functions}{% Composition of Continuous Functions}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
%
  1. Fix $\varepsilon > 0$. Apply continuity of $g$ at $f(x_0)$: get
     $\delta_1 > 0$ with $|y - f(x_0)| < \delta_1 \Rightarrow
     |g(y) - g(f(x_0))| < \varepsilon$.\\
  2. Set $\varepsilon_0 := \delta_1$. Apply continuity of $f$ at $x_0$:
     get $\delta_0 > 0$ with $|x - x_0| < \delta_0 \Rightarrow
     |f(x) - f(x_0)| < \delta_1$.\\
  3. Chain: $|x - x_0| < \delta_0 \Rightarrow |f(x) - f(x_0)| <
     \delta_1 \Rightarrow |h(x) - h(x_0)| < \varepsilon$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: % 1. Fix ε > 0. Apply continuity of g at f(x_0): get _1 > 0 with |y - f(x_0)| 0 with |x - x_0| < _0 ⇒ |f(x) - f(x_0)| < _1.\\ 3. Chain: |x - x_0| < _0 ⇒ |f(x) - f(x_0)| < _1 ⇒ |h(x) - h(x_0)| < ε.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:continuity-three-equiv
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:continuity-three-equiv]
{%
    Three Equivalent Forms of Continuity}}{%
    Three Equivalent Forms of Continuity}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:continuity-three-equiv] {% Three Equivalent Forms of Continuity}{% Three Equivalent Forms of Continuity}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
State the three equivalent forms of continuity of $f$
  at $c \in A$.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: State the three equivalent forms of continuity of f at c A.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
(i) $\varepsilon$-$\delta$: $\forall\varepsilon>0\,
  \exists\delta>0\,\forall x\in A:\,|x-c|<\delta\Rightarrow
  |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon$.
  (ii) Neighbourhood: $f(A\cap V_\delta(c))\subseteq
  V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
  (iii) Sequential: $x_n\to c\Rightarrow f(x_n)\to f(c)$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: (i) ε- : ε>0\, >0\, x A:\,|x-c|< ⇒ |f(x)-f(c)|<ε. (ii) Neighbourhood: f(A V_ (c))⊆ V_ε(f(c)). (iii) Sequential: x_n→ c⇒ f(x_n)→ f(c).
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
(i)$\iff$(ii) is notation only.
  (i)$\iff$(iii) is the continuity analogue of Bartle 4.1.8,
  but sequences may now include $x_n = c$.

Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:continuity-three-equiv]
{%
    Three Equivalent Forms of Continuity}}{%

Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:continuity-three-equiv] {% Three Equivalent Forms of Continuity}{% Three Equivalent Forms of Continuity}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Sketch the proof of: \texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:continuity-three-equiv]
{%
    Three Equivalent Forms of Continuity}}{%
    Three Equivalent Forms of Continuity}.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: % \hyperref[prf:continuity-three-equiv] {% Three Equivalent Forms of Continuity}{% Three Equivalent Forms of Continuity}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
1. (i)$\Rightarrow$(ii): Fix $\varepsilon_0$, get $\delta_0$
from (i). Any $x_0 \in A \cap V_{\delta_0}(c)$ satisfies
$f(x_0) \in V_{\varepsilon_0}(f(c))$; arbitrariness gives the inclusion.
2. (ii)$\Rightarrow$(iii): Fix $\varepsilon$, get $\delta$ from (ii).
Sequential characterisation gives $N$ with $x_n \in V_\delta(c)$ for
$n \geq N$; neighbourhood condition pulls $f(x_n) \in V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
3. (iii)$\Rightarrow$(i): Contrapositive. Failure of (i) at $\varepsilon_0$
yields $(x_n)$ via $\delta = 1/n$ with $x_n \to c$ but
$f(x_n) \not\to f(c)$, contradicting~(iii).
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: 1. (i)⇒(ii): Fix ε_0, get _0 from (i). Any x_0 A V_{ _0}(c) satisfies f(x_0) V_{ε_0}(f(c)); arbitrariness gives the inclusion. 2. (ii)⇒(iii): Fix ε, get from (ii). Sequential characterisation gives N with x_n V_ (c) for n ≥ N; neighbourhood condition pulls f(x_n) V_ε(f(c)). 3. (iii)⇒(i): Contrapositive. Failure of (i) at ε_0 yields (x_n) via = 1/n with x_n → c but f(x_n) → f(c), contradicting~(iii).

Analysis · Continuity.label: prop:continuous-injective-iff-strictly-monotone
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:continuous-injective-iff-strictly-monotone]
{Continuous Injective Map on a Closed Interval Is Strictly Monotone}}{%
  Continuous Injective Map on a Closed Interval Is Strictly Monotone}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:continuous-injective-iff-strictly-monotone] {Continuous Injective Map on a Closed Interval Is Strictly Monotone}{% Continuous Injective Map on a Closed Interval Is Strictly Monotone}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: When is a continuous function on $[a,b]$ injective?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: When is a continuous function on [a,b] injective?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
Exactly when it is strictly monotone. Continuity plus
  injectivity forces strict monotonicity; conversely strict
  monotonicity implies injectivity.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: Exactly when it is strictly monotone. Continuity plus injectivity forces strict monotonicity; conversely strict monotonicity implies injectivity.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Non-monotonicity forces a local extremum; IVT then
  produces two preimages of some value, contradicting injectivity.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:continuous-inverse-theorem
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:continuous-inverse-theorem]
{Continuous Inverse Theorem}}{%
  Continuous Inverse Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:continuous-inverse-theorem] {Continuous Inverse Theorem}{% Continuous Inverse Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: When is the inverse of a function continuous?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: When is the inverse of a function continuous?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
If $f$ is strictly monotone and continuous on an interval $I$,
  then $f^{-1}$ is strictly monotone and continuous on $f(I)$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: If f is strictly monotone and continuous on an interval I, then f^{-1} is strictly monotone and continuous on f(I).
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Continuity of $f$ + Preservation of Intervals ensures
  $f(I)$ is an interval with no gaps; a jump in $f^{-1}$ would
  create a gap in $f(I)$.

Analysis · Continuity.label: prop:criterion-for-continuity-of-monotone-function
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:criterion-for-continuity-of-monotone-function]
{Criterion for Continuity of a Monotone Function}}{%
  Criterion for Continuity of a Monotone Function}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:criterion-for-continuity-of-monotone-function] {Criterion for Continuity of a Monotone Function}{% Criterion for Continuity of a Monotone Function}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: When is a monotone function on $[a,b]$ continuous?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: When is a monotone function on [a,b] continuous?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
Exactly when $f([a,b])$ is the closed interval $[f(a),f(b)]$
  (or $[f(b),f(a)]$ if decreasing) — no gaps in the range.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: Exactly when f([a,b]) is the closed interval [f(a),f(b)] (or [f(b),f(a)] if decreasing) — no gaps in the range.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Each jump discontinuity creates a gap; no gaps means
  no jumps means continuous.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:global-continuity-theorem
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:global-continuity-theorem]
{Global Continuity Theorem}}{%
  Global Continuity Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:global-continuity-theorem] {Global Continuity Theorem}{% Global Continuity Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: State the Global Continuity Theorem.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: State the Global Continuity Theorem.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous iff preimages of
  closed sets are closed, iff preimages of open sets are open.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: f : ℝ → ℝ is continuous iff preimages of closed sets are closed, iff preimages of open sets are open.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Continuity = preimage preserves open/closed structure.
  This is the topological definition of continuity.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:injective-continuous-is-monotone

  \hyperref[prf:injective-continuous-is-monotone]
{Injective Continuous Function Is Strictly Monotone}}{%
  Injective Continuous Function Is Strictly Monotone}

Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
If $f$ is continuous and injective on an interval, what
  can be said?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: If f is continuous and injective on an interval, what can be said?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f$ must be strictly monotone — either strictly increasing
  or strictly decreasing throughout.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: f must be strictly monotone — either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing throughout.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Non-monotonicity forces a local max or min, which IVT
  turns into a repeated value, contradicting injectivity.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:inverse-function-theorem-monotone
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:inverse-function-theorem-monotone]
{Inverse Function Theorem for Continuous Strictly Monotone Functions}}{%
  Inverse Function Theorem for Continuous Strictly Monotone Functions}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:inverse-function-theorem-monotone] {Inverse Function Theorem for Continuous Strictly Monotone Functions}{% Inverse Function Theorem for Continuous Strictly Monotone Functions}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
State the Inverse Function Theorem for continuous strictly
  monotone functions on $[a,b]$.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: State the Inverse Function Theorem for continuous strictly monotone functions on [a,b].
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f$ strictly monotone and continuous on $[a,b]$ implies
  $f([a,b]) = [f(a),f(b)]$ and $f^{-1}$ is continuous and strictly
  monotone on $f([a,b])$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: f strictly monotone and continuous on [a,b] implies f([a,b]) = [f(a),f(b)] and f^{-1} is continuous and strictly monotone on f([a,b]).
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Three conclusions: (1) inverse exists; (2) image is
  a closed interval; (3) inverse is continuous.

  $f^{-1}$?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: If f is strictly monotone on X, what can be said about f^{-1}?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f^{-1}$ exists on $f(X)$ and has the same monotonicity type
  as $f$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: f^{-1} exists on f(X) and has the same monotonicity type as f.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Strict monotonicity gives injectivity; the inverse then
  inherits the order direction.

Analysis · Continuity.label: cor:jump-intervals-for-monotone-discontinuities
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:jump-intervals-for-monotone-discontinuities]
{Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint}}{%
  Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:jump-intervals-for-monotone-discontinuities] {Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint}{% Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
What is the jump interval at a discontinuity of a monotone
  function, and why are they disjoint?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What is the jump interval at a discontinuity of a monotone function, and why are they disjoint?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
The jump interval at $a$ is $(f(a^-), f(a^+))$ — the gap
  in the range. Distinct discontinuities produce disjoint jump
  intervals because monotonicity forces the ranges to be ordered.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: The jump interval at a is (f(a^-), f(a^+)) — the gap in the range. Distinct discontinuities produce disjoint jump intervals because monotonicity forces the ranges to be ordered.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Monotonicity separates the jump intervals: $a < b$
  forces the jump at $a$ below the jump at $b$ with no overlap.

Analysis · Continuity.label: cor:jump-intervals-for-monotone-discontinuities
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:jump-intervals-for-monotone-discontinuities]
{Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint}}{%
  Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:jump-intervals-for-monotone-discontinuities] {Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint}{% Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Sketch the proof of: \texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:jump-intervals-for-monotone-discontinuities]
{Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint}}{%
  Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint}.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: % \hyperref[prf:jump-intervals-for-monotone-discontinuities] {Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint}{% Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
1. WLOG $f$ increasing, $c < d$. 2. For any $x \in (c,d)$:
$f(c^+) \leq f(x) \leq f(d^-)$ by monotonicity. 3. Hence $f(c^+) \leq f(d^-)$.
4. Every element of $J_c(f)$ is $< f(c^+)$ and every element of $J_d(f)$ is
$> f(d^-)$, so the intervals are disjoint.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: 1. WLOG f increasing, c f(d^-), so the intervals are disjoint.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:local-properties-of-continuous-functions
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:local-properties-of-continuous-functions]
{Local Properties of Continuous Functions}}{%
  Local Properties of Continuous Functions}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:local-properties-of-continuous-functions] {Local Properties of Continuous Functions}{% Local Properties of Continuous Functions}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: What four local properties does continuity at a point guarantee?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What four local properties does continuity at a point guarantee?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
(1) Local boundedness near $a$. (2) Sign stability near $a$
  when $f(a)\neq 0$. (3) Algebra: sums, differences, products,
  quotients (when denominator $\neq 0$) are continuous at $a$.
  (4) Composition with a function continuous at $f(a)$ is continuous
  at $a$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: (1) Local boundedness near a. (2) Sign stability near a when f(a)≠ 0. (3) Algebra: sums, differences, products, quotients (when denominator ≠ 0) are continuous at a. (4) Composition with a function continuous at f(a) is continuous at a.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Zorich bundles four separate results into one theorem.
  Each part corresponds to a theorem elsewhere in the notes.

Analysis · Continuity.label: cor:monotone-continuity-criterion
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:monotone-continuity-criterion]
{Continuity Criterion for Monotone Functions}}{%
  Continuity Criterion for Monotone Functions}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:monotone-continuity-criterion] {Continuity Criterion for Monotone Functions}{% Continuity Criterion for Monotone Functions}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
When is an increasing function continuous at an interior
  point $c$?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: When is an increasing function continuous at an interior point c?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
Exactly when the left and right limits both equal $f(c)$,
  equivalently when $\sup\{f(x): x < c\} = f(c) =
  \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: Exactly when the left and right limits both equal f(c), equivalently when \{f(x): x c\}.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
No gap between the left sup and the right inf at $c$ —
  the jump is zero.

$f$ continuous at $c$
$\;\iff\;$
$f(c^-) = f(c) = f(c^+)$
$\;\iff\;$
$\sup\{f(x): x < c\} = f(c) = \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: f continuous at c \;iff\; f(c^-) = f(c) = f(c^+) \;iff\; \{f(x): x c\}.

Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: % \hyperref[prf:monotone-continuity-criterion] {Continuity Criterion for Monotone Functions}{% Continuity Criterion for Monotone Functions}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
1. $(\Rightarrow)$: continuity gives two-sided limit, so both one-sided
limits exist and equal $f(c)$; in particular they are equal. 2. $(\Leftarrow)$:
one-sided limits exist by \#116; ordering $f(c^{-}) \leq f(c) \leq f(c^{+})$ plus
$f(c^{-}) = f(c^{+})$ forces all three equal; hence two-sided limit $= f(c)$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: 1. (⇒): continuity gives two-sided limit, so both one-sided limits exist and equal f(c); in particular they are equal. 2. ( ): one-sided limits exist by \#116; ordering f(c^{-}) ≤ f(c) ≤ f(c^{+}) plus f(c^{-}) = f(c^{+}) forces all three equal; hence two-sided limit = f(c).

Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: Where is a monotone function continuous?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Where is a monotone function continuous?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
At a set dense in its domain. The discontinuity set is
  countable, hence nowhere dense, so its complement is dense.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: At a set dense in its domain. The discontinuity set is countable, hence nowhere dense, so its complement is dense.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Countable $\Rightarrow$ nowhere dense $\Rightarrow$
  complement is dense.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:monotone-discontinuities-countable

Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: How many discontinuities can a monotone function have?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: How many discontinuities can a monotone function have?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
At most countably many. The jump intervals at distinct
  discontinuities are disjoint; each contains a rational; density
  of $\mathbb{Q}$ gives the injection into $\mathbb{Q}$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: At most countably many. The jump intervals at distinct discontinuities are disjoint; each contains a rational; density of ℚ gives the injection into ℚ.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Disjoint intervals $\hookrightarrow$ distinct rationals
  $\hookrightarrow$ countable.

Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: % \hyperref[prf:monotone-discontinuities-countable] {Discontinuities of a Monotone Function Are Countable}{% Discontinuities of a Monotone Function Are Countable}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
1. WLOG $f$ increasing. 2. Each $c \in D$ gives non-empty
$J_c(f) = (f(c^-), f(c^+))$; pick $r(c) \in J_c(f) \cap \mathbb{Q}$ by density.
3. Jump intervals are pairwise disjoint (Jump Intervals corollary), so $r$ is
injective. 4. Injection $D \hookrightarrow \mathbb{Q}$, $\mathbb{Q}$ countable,
so $D$ at most countable.

Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: What kind of discontinuities can a monotone function have?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What kind of discontinuities can a monotone function have?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
Only discontinuities of first kind — both one-sided limits
  exist at every discontinuity. Oscillation is impossible for a
  monotone function.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: Only discontinuities of first kind — both one-sided limits exist at every discontinuity. Oscillation is impossible for a monotone function.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Monotonicity forces all nearby values to lie on the same
  side, so one-sided limits must converge. No oscillation is possible.

Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: % \hyperref[prf:monotone-one-sided-limits] {One-Sided Limits of Monotone Functions}{% One-Sided Limits of Monotone Functions}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
1. Set $s = \sup S$, $j = \inf J$; both finite by boundedness from interior
point. 2. For $\lim_{x \to c^{-}}$: $s - \varepsilon$ not an upper bound gives $x_0 
c$; take $\delta = c - x_0$; monotonicity pins $f(x) \in (s-\varepsilon, s]$. 3.
Right-hand limit is the mirror via $\inf J$. 4. Ordering follows from monotonicity
applied to all $x < c$ (resp.\ $x > c$).
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: 1. Set s = S, j = J; both finite by boundedness from interior point. 2. For _{x → c^{-}}: s - ε not an upper bound gives x_0 c; take = c - x_0; monotonicity pins f(x) (s-ε, s]. 3. Right-hand limit is the mirror via J. 4. Ordering follows from monotonicity applied to all x c).

Analysis · Continuity.label: prop:non-uniform-continuity-criteria
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:non-uniform-continuity-criteria]
{Non-Uniform Continuity Criteria}}{%
  Non-Uniform Continuity Criteria}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:non-uniform-continuity-criteria] {Non-Uniform Continuity Criteria}{% Non-Uniform Continuity Criteria}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: State the sequential criterion for failure of uniform continuity.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: State the sequential criterion for failure of uniform continuity.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f$ fails to be uniformly continuous on $A$ iff there exist
  $\varepsilon_0 > 0$ and sequences $(x_n),(u_n) \subseteq A$ with
  $x_n - u_n \to 0$ but $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)| \geq \varepsilon_0$ for all $n$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: f fails to be uniformly continuous on A iff there exist ε_0 > 0 and sequences (x_n),(u_n) ⊆ A with x_n - u_n → 0 but |f(x_n)-f(u_n)| ≥ ε_0 for all n.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Inputs squeeze together; outputs stay apart. This is the
  negation of uniform continuity with $\delta = 1/n$.

Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f(x)=x^2$ on $\mathbb{R}$: take $x_n = n+1/n$,
  $u_n = n$; then $x_n - u_n = 1/n \to 0$ but
  $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)| = 2 + 1/n^2 \not\to 0$.

Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: % \hyperref[prf:non-uniform-continuity-criteria] {Non-Uniform Continuity Criteria}{% Non-Uniform Continuity Criteria}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
%
  $(\Rightarrow)$: Negate UC; set $\delta=1/n$ to get $(x_n),(u_n)$ with
  $|x_n-u_n|<1/n$ and $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)|\geq\varepsilon_0$. Squeeze gives
  $x_n-u_n\to 0$. \\
  $(\Leftarrow)$: Suppose UC holds; get $\delta>0$. Since $x_n-u_n\to 0$,
  pick $N$ with $|x_N-u_N|<\delta$, giving $|f(x_N)-f(u_N)|<\varepsilon_0$.
  Contradicts $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)|\geq\varepsilon_0$ for all $n$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: % (⇒): Negate UC; set =1/n to get (x_n),(u_n) with |x_n-u_n| 0. Since x_n-u_n→ 0, pick N with |x_N-u_N|< , giving |f(x_N)-f(u_N)|<ε_0. Contradicts |f(x_n)-f(u_n)|≥ε_0 for all n.

Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: State the Piecewise Linear Approximation Theorem.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: State the Piecewise Linear Approximation Theorem.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
Every continuous $f$ on $[a,b]$ can be approximated uniformly
  to within $\varepsilon$ by a piecewise linear function.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: Every continuous f on [a,b] can be approximated uniformly to within ε by a piecewise linear function.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Heine–Cantor gives $\delta$; partition $[a,b]$ into
  pieces of width $< \delta$; connect the graph values at the nodes
  by straight line segments.

Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: % \hyperref[prf:power-function-continuous] {Power Function Is Continuous}{% Power Function Is Continuous}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
%
  1. Base case $n=1$: set $\delta := \varepsilon$; then
     $|x - x_0| < \delta \Rightarrow |x - x_0| < \varepsilon$. \\
  2. Inductive step: $x^{n+1} = x^n \cdot x$; product of two continuous
     functions (IH + base case) is continuous by the product rule. \\
  3. Conclude by induction.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: % 1. Base case n=1: set := ε; then |x - x_0| < ⇒ |x - x_0| < ε. \\ 2. Inductive step: x^{n+1} = x^n x; product of two continuous functions (IH + base case) is continuous by the product rule. \\ 3. Conclude by induction.

Analysis · Continuity.label: prop:rational-functions-continuous
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:rational-functions-continuous]
{Rational Functions Are Continuous on Their Domain}}{%
  Rational Functions Are Continuous on Their Domain}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:rational-functions-continuous] {Rational Functions Are Continuous on Their Domain}{% Rational Functions Are Continuous on Their Domain}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: State the continuity theorem for rational functions.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: State the continuity theorem for rational functions.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
If $P$ and $Q$ are real polynomials and $D = \{x \in \mathbb{R} : Q(x) \neq 0\}$,
  then $R(x) = P(x)/Q(x)$ is continuous on $D$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: If P and Q are real polynomials and D = \{x ℝ : Q(x) ≠ 0\}, then R(x) = P(x)/Q(x) is continuous on D.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Polynomials are continuous everywhere; quotient rule for continuous
  functions applies whenever the denominator is nonzero.

Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
$\forall$ real polynomials $P$, $Q$,
$\forall x_0 \in D = \{x \in \mathbb{R} : Q(x) \neq 0\}$:
$R = P/Q$ is continuous at $x_0$.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: real polynomials P, Q, x_0 D = \{x ℝ : Q(x) ≠ 0\}: R = P/Q is continuous at x_0.

Analysis · Continuity.label: prop:sequential-uniform-continuity
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:sequential-uniform-continuity]
{Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity}}{%
  Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:sequential-uniform-continuity] {Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity}{% Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: State the sequential characterisation of uniform continuity.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: State the sequential characterisation of uniform continuity.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f$ is uniformly continuous iff whenever $(x_n) \sim (y_n)$
  in $X$ (i.e., $x_n - y_n \to 0$), we have $f(x_n) - f(y_n) \to 0$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: f is uniformly continuous iff whenever (x_n) (y_n) in X (i.e., x_n - y_n → 0), we have f(x_n) - f(y_n) → 0.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Equivalent inputs produce equivalent outputs. Compare with
  pointwise continuity where only one sequence moves toward a fixed
  point.

Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f(x)=1/x$ on $(0,1)$: take $x_n = 1/n$, $y_n = 1/2n$;
  then $x_n - y_n \to 0$ but $f(x_n)-f(y_n) = n - 2n = -n \not\to 0$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: f(x)=1/x on (0,1): take x_n = 1/n, y_n = 1/2n; then x_n - y_n → 0 but f(x_n)-f(y_n) = n - 2n = -n → 0.

Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:sequential-uniform-continuity]
{Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity}}{%
  Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:sequential-uniform-continuity] {Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity}{% Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Sketch the proof of: \texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:sequential-uniform-continuity]
{Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity}}{%
  Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity}.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: % \hyperref[prf:sequential-uniform-continuity] {Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity}{% Sequential Characterization of Uniform Continuity}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
%
  $(\Rightarrow)$: UC gives $\delta$ for $\varepsilon$. Since $|x_n-y_n|\to 0$,
  pick $N$ with $|x_n-y_n|<\delta$ for $n>N$. Then $|f(x_n)-f(y_n)|<\varepsilon$
  for all $n>N$. As $\varepsilon$ was arbitrary, $|f(x_n)-f(y_n)|\to 0$. \\
  $(\Leftarrow)$: If $f$ not UC, Non-Uniform Continuity Criteria gives
  $(x_n),(y_n)$ with $|x_n-y_n|\to 0$ but $|f(x_n)-f(y_n)|\geq\varepsilon_0$
  for all $n$ — contradicting the hypothesis.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: % (⇒): UC gives for ε. Since |x_n-y_n|→ 0, pick N with |x_n-y_n| N. Then |f(x_n)-f(y_n)| N. As ε was arbitrary, |f(x_n)-f(y_n)|→ 0. \\ ( ): If f not UC, Non-Uniform Continuity Criteria gives (x_n),(y_n) with |x_n-y_n|→ 0 but |f(x_n)-f(y_n)|≥ε_0 for all n — contradicting the hypothesis.

Analysis · Continuity.label: prop:sign-preservation
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:sign-preservation]
{Sign Preservation}}{%
  Sign Preservation}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:sign-preservation] {Sign Preservation}{% Sign Preservation}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: State Sign Preservation for continuous functions.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: State Sign Preservation for continuous functions.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
If $f$ is continuous at $x_0$ and $f(x_0) \neq 0$, then $f$ preserves
  the sign of $f(x_0)$ on some neighbourhood of $x_0$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: If f is continuous at x_0 and f(x_0) ≠ 0, then f preserves the sign of f(x_0) on some neighbourhood of x_0.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Apply continuity with $\varepsilon = |f(x_0)|/2$; the resulting
  $\delta$-neighbourhood keeps $f$ bounded away from zero on the same side.

Analysis · Continuity.label: prop:sign-preservation

  Sign Preservation}

Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
$f$ continuous at $x_0$, $f(x_0) > 0$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$(\exists\,\delta > 0)(\forall x \in E)\;|x - x_0| < \delta \Rightarrow f(x) > 0$.

  \hyperref[prf:sign-preservation]
{Sign Preservation}}{%

Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: % \hyperref[prf:sign-preservation] {Sign Preservation}{% Sign Preservation}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
%
  1. Set $\varepsilon_0 = |f(x_0)| > 0$; continuity gives $\delta$ with
     $|x-x_0|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(x_0)| < |f(x_0)|$. \\
  2. Unpack: $f(x_0) - |f(x_0)| < f(x) < f(x_0) + |f(x_0)|$. \\
  3. If $f(x_0)>0$: lower bound gives $f(x) > 0$.
     If $f(x_0)<0$: upper bound gives $f(x) < 0$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: % 1. Set ε_0 = |f(x_0)| > 0; continuity gives with |x-x_0| 0: lower bound gives f(x) > 0. If f(x_0)<0: upper bound gives f(x) < 0.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:step-function-approximation

{Step Function Approximation}}{%
  Step Function Approximation}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:step-function-approximation] {Step Function Approximation}{% Step Function Approximation}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
What does uniform continuity enable for continuous functions
  on $[a,b]$?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What does uniform continuity enable for continuous functions on [a,b]?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
Any continuous $f$ on $[a,b]$ can be approximated uniformly
  to within $\varepsilon$ by a step function.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: Any continuous f on [a,b] can be approximated uniformly to within ε by a step function.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Heine–Cantor gives $\delta$; partition $[a,b]$ into
  pieces of width $< \delta$; constant approximation on each piece
  is within $\varepsilon$.

Analysis · Continuity.label: cor:uc-bijection-compact-inverse-uc
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:uc-bijection-compact-inverse-uc]
{Uniformly Continuous Bijection on a Compact Domain Has Uniformly Continuous Inverse}}{%
  Uniformly Continuous Bijection on a Compact Domain Has Uniformly Continuous Inverse}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:uc-bijection-compact-inverse-uc] {Uniformly Continuous Bijection on a Compact Domain Has Uniformly Continuous Inverse}{% Uniformly Continuous Bijection on a Compact Domain Has Uniformly Continuous Inverse}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
If $f : [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is a continuous bijection,
  is $f^{-1}$ uniformly continuous?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: If f : [a,b] → ℝ is a continuous bijection, is f^{-1} uniformly continuous?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
Yes. $f^{-1}$ is continuous by the Continuous Inverse
  Theorem, and UC by Heine–Cantor since $f([a,b])$ is a compact
  interval.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: Yes. f^{-1} is continuous by the Continuous Inverse Theorem, and UC by Heine–Cantor since f([a,b]) is a compact interval.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Two-step: CIT gives continuity of $f^{-1}$, then
  Heine–Cantor promotes it to UC on the compact image.

  continuity.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:uc-space-is-convex
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{%
  \hyperref[prf:uc-space-is-convex]
{The Space of Uniformly Continuous Functions Is Convex}}{%
  The Space of Uniformly Continuous Functions Is Convex}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:uc-space-is-convex] {The Space of Uniformly Continuous Functions Is Convex}{% The Space of Uniformly Continuous Functions Is Convex}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: Is the set of uniformly continuous functions on $A$ convex?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Is the set of uniformly continuous functions on A convex?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
Yes. If $f_1, f_2$ are UC and $t \in [0,1]$, then
  $(1-t)f_1 + tf_2$ is UC. Proof: one-line consequence of the
  algebra of uniform continuity.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: Yes. If f_1, f_2 are UC and t [0,1], then (1-t)f_1 + tf_2 is UC. Proof: one-line consequence of the algebra of uniform continuity.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Convex combination is a linear combination with
  non-negative coefficients summing to 1. The algebra theorem
  gives UC for any linear combination, so convexity is immediate.

Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: % \hyperref[prf:uc-space-is-convex] {The Space of Uniformly Continuous Functions Is Convex}{% The Space of Uniformly Continuous Functions Is Convex}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:

  \hyperref[prf:uc-space-is-convex]
{The Space of Uniformly Continuous Functions Is Convex}}{%
  The Space of Uniformly Continuous Functions Is Convex}.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Give a failure case or negation for: % \hyperref[prf:uc-space-is-convex] {The Space of Uniformly Continuous Functions Is Convex}{% The Space of Uniformly Continuous Functions Is Convex}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
The set of Lipschitz functions with a \emph{fixed}
  constant $K$ is also convex (the constant of the combination
  is at most $K$), but the set of all Lipschitz functions
  (varying constants) is a vector space, not just convex.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: The set of Lipschitz functions with a fixed constant K is also convex (the constant of the combination is at most K), but the set of all Lipschitz functions (varying constants) is a vector space, not just convex.

  can be said about $f(X)$?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: If f is uniformly continuous on a bounded set X, what can be said about f(X)?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f(X)$ is bounded. Uniform continuity controls oscillation
  globally; boundedness of $X$ means finitely many $\delta$-patches
  suffice.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: f(X) is bounded. Uniform continuity controls oscillation globally; boundedness of X means finitely many -patches suffice.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Continuous but not uniformly continuous on a bounded set:
  $1/x$ on $(0,1)$. Uniform continuity rules this out.

Analysis · Continuity.label: cor:uniform-continuity-limit-at-adherent
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:

Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: What does uniform continuity guarantee at an adherent point?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What does uniform continuity guarantee at an adherent point?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
If $f$ is uniformly continuous on $X$ and $x_0$ is adherent
  to $X$, then $\lim_{x \to x_0} f(x)$ exists.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: If f is uniformly continuous on X and x_0 is adherent to X, then _{x → x_0} f(x) exists.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Any sequence approaching $x_0$ is Cauchy; UC maps it to a
  Cauchy sequence; Cauchy sequences converge.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:bolzano-intermediate-value
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:bolzano-intermediate-value]
{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}}{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:bolzano-intermediate-value] {Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: State Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: State Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: If \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on an interval \(I\), and if \(a,b\in I\) with \(f(a)<k<f(b)\), then there exists a point \(c\) between \(a\) and \(b\) such that \(f(c)=k\).
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: If \(f:I→ℝ\) is continuous on an interval \(I\), and if \(a,b I\) with \(f(a)<k<f(b)\), then there exists a point \(c\) between \(a\) and \(b\) such that \(f(c)=k\).
Analysis · Continuity.hint: Subtract \(k\) and reduce to the Location of Roots Theorem.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:bolzano-intermediate-value
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:bolzano-intermediate-value]
{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}}{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:bolzano-intermediate-value] {Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Let \(I\subseteq\mathbb{R}\) be an interval and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\ \wedge\
a\in I
\ \wedge\
b\in I
\ \wedge\
f(a)<k<f(b)
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in I
\text{ such that }
\min\{a,b\}<c<\max\{a,b\}
\text{ and }
f(c)=k.
\]
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Let \(I⊆ℝ\) be an interval and let \(f:I→ℝ\). Then \[ f continuous on I \ \ a I \ \ b I \ \ f(a)<k<f(b) \] implies \[ c I such that \{a,b\}<c< \{a,b\} and f(c)=k. \]
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: This is the Theorem: \hyperref[prf:bolzano-intermediate-value] {Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : {Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:bolzano-intermediate-value
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:bolzano-intermediate-value]
{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}}{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:bolzano-intermediate-value] {Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Sketch the proof of: \texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:bolzano-intermediate-value]
{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}}{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: \hyperref[prf:bolzano-intermediate-value] {Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}{Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
Set $g = f - k$. Continuity of $g$ is free from algebra of
continuous functions. $g(a) < 0 < g(b)$ by hypothesis. Location of
Roots gives $c$ between $a$ and $b$ with $g(c) = 0$, i.e., $f(c) = k$.
Second case: apply to $-f$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: Set g = f - k. Continuity of g is free from algebra of continuous functions. g(a) < 0 < g(b) by hypothesis. Location of Roots gives c between a and b with g(c) = 0, i.e., f(c) = k. Second case: apply to -f.

{Boundedness Theorem}}{Boundedness Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:boundedness-theorem] {Boundedness Theorem}{Boundedness Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: What does continuity on a closed bounded interval guarantee first?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What does continuity on a closed bounded interval guarantee first?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) is bounded on \([a,b]\).
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: If \(f:[a,b]→ℝ\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) is bounded on \([a,b]\).
Analysis · Continuity.hint: This is the first compactness consequence for continuous functions on closed intervals.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:boundedness-theorem
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:boundedness-theorem]
{Boundedness Theorem}}{Boundedness Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:boundedness-theorem] {Boundedness Theorem}{Boundedness Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
For all \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\exists M>0\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ |f(x)|\le M.
\]
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: For all \(a,b ℝ\) with \(a b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]→ℝ\), \[ f continuous on [a,b] M>0\; x ,\ |f(x)| M. \]
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: This is the Theorem: \hyperref[prf:boundedness-theorem] {Boundedness Theorem}{Boundedness Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : {Boundedness Theorem}{Boundedness Theorem}

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:boundedness-theorem
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:boundedness-theorem]
{Boundedness Theorem}}{Boundedness Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:boundedness-theorem] {Boundedness Theorem}{Boundedness Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Sketch the proof of: \texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:boundedness-theorem]
{Boundedness Theorem}}{Boundedness Theorem}.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: \hyperref[prf:boundedness-theorem] {Boundedness Theorem}{Boundedness Theorem}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
%
  1. Assume $f$ unbounded: choose $x_n \in I$ with $|f(x_n)| > n$. \\
  2. $(x_n) \subseteq [a,b]$ bounded $\xrightarrow{\text{B-W}}$
     subsequence $x_{n_k} \to x^* \in I$ (closed). \\
  3. Continuity: $f(x_{n_k}) \to f(x^*)$, so image subsequence bounded;
     but $|f(x_{n_k})| > n_k \to \infty$. Contradiction.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: % 1. Assume f unbounded: choose x_n I with |f(x_n)| > n. \\ 2. (x_n) ⊆ [a,b] bounded \text{B-W} subsequence x_{n_k} → x^* I (closed). \\ 3. Continuity: f(x_{n_k}) → f(x^*), so image subsequence bounded; but |f(x_{n_k})| > n_k → ∞. Contradiction.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:continuity-algebra-at-a-point
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:continuity-algebra-at-a-point]
{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}}{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:continuity-algebra-at-a-point] {Algebra of Continuity at a Point}{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: Which algebraic operations preserve continuity at a point?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Which algebraic operations preserve continuity at a point?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: If \(f\) and \(g\) are continuous at \(c\), then \(f+g\), \(f-g\), \(fg\), and \(bf\) are continuous at \(c\). If \(h\) is continuous at \(c\) and never vanishes on \(A\), then \(f/h\) is continuous at \(c\).
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: If \(f\) and \(g\) are continuous at \(c\), then \(f+g\), \(f-g\), \(fg\), and \(bf\) are continuous at \(c\). If \(h\) is continuous at \(c\) and never vanishes on \(A\), then \(f/h\) is continuous at \(c\).
Analysis · Continuity.hint: Reduce continuity to limits at the point, then apply the algebra of limits of functions.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:continuity-algebra-at-a-point
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:continuity-algebra-at-a-point]
{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}}{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:continuity-algebra-at-a-point] {Algebra of Continuity at a Point}{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), let \(b \in \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in A\).
  1. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ g \text{ is continuous at } c\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f+g,\ f-g,\ fg,\ bf \text{ are continuous at } c\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ h \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ \forall x\in A,\ h(x)\neq 0\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f/h \text{ is continuous at } c\bigr). \]
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Let \(A ⊆ ℝ\), let \(f,g : A → ℝ\), let \(b ℝ\), and let \(c A\). \[ (f is continuous at c \ \ g is continuous at c ) ⇒ (f+g,\ f-g,\ fg,\ bf are continuous at c ). \] \[ (f is continuous at c \ \ h is continuous at c \ \ x A,\ h(x)≠ 0 ) ⇒ (f/h is continuous at c ). \]
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: This is the Theorem: \hyperref[prf:continuity-algebra-at-a-point] {Algebra of Continuity at a Point}{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : {Algebra of Continuity at a Point}{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:continuity-algebra-at-a-point
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:continuity-algebra-at-a-point]
{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}}{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:continuity-algebra-at-a-point] {Algebra of Continuity at a Point}{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Sketch the proof of: \texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:continuity-algebra-at-a-point]
{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}}{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: \hyperref[prf:continuity-algebra-at-a-point] {Algebra of Continuity at a Point}{Algebra of Continuity at a Point}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: 1. Rewrite continuity at c as the limit identities lim_{x->c} f(x)=f(c) and lim_{x->c} g(x)=g(c). 2. Apply the sum, difference, product, and scalar-multiple rules for limits to obtain continuity of f+g, f-g, fg, and bf at c. 3. For the quotient part, use continuity of h at c together with h(x) != 0 on A to get h(c) != 0, then apply the quotient rule for limits.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: 1. Rewrite continuity at c as the limit identities lim_{x->c} f(x)=f(c) and lim_{x->c} g(x)=g(c). 2. Apply the sum, difference, product, and scalar-multiple rules for limits to obtain continuity of f+g, f-g, fg, and bf at c. 3. For the quotient part, use continuity of h at c together with h(x) != 0 on A to get h(c) != 0, then apply the quotient rule for limits.

Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: 1. Fix an arbitrary c in A. 2. Since f and g are continuous on A, they are continuous at c. Apply the pointwise algebra-of-continuity theorem to obtain continuity of f+g, f-g, fg, and bf at c. 3. For the quotient part, use that h is continuous on A and nonzero on A, hence continuous at c and nonzero there. Apply the pointwise quotient result. 4. Since c was arbitrary, the derived functions are continuous on A.

Since c was arbitrary, the derived functions are continuous on A.

Analysis · Continuity.label: lem:discontinuity-on-a-set
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:discontinuity-on-a-set]
{Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere}}{Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:discontinuity-on-a-set] {Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere}{Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: How is discontinuity on a set related to pointwise discontinuity?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: How is discontinuity on a set related to pointwise discontinuity?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is discontinuous on \(E\) if and only if there exists a point \(x_0\in E\) where it is discontinuous. By the equivalence lemma, the bad point may be witnessed in epsilon-delta, sequential, or neighborhood form.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: A function \(f:E→ℝ\) is discontinuous on \(E\) if and only if there exists a point \(x_0 E\) where it is discontinuous. By the equivalence lemma, the bad point may be witnessed in epsilon-delta, sequential, or neighborhood form.
Analysis · Continuity.hint: Set-level discontinuity is just one outer existential quantifier added to the pointwise notion.

Analysis · Continuity.label: lem:discontinuity-on-a-set
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:discontinuity-on-a-set]
{Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere}}{Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:discontinuity-on-a-set] {Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere}{Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\). Then the following are equivalent:
  1. \[ f \text{ is discontinuous on } E. \]
  2. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E: \bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr). \]
  3. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists (x_n)\subseteq E: \quad x_n\to x_0 \quad\text{and}\quad f(x_n)\not\to f(x_0). \]
  4. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists V \text{ a neighborhood of } f(x_0)\; \forall U \text{ a neighborhood of } x_0\; \exists x\in U\cap E: \quad f(x)\notin V. \]
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Let \(E ⊆ ℝ\) and let \(f : E → ℝ\). Then the following are equivalent: \[ f is discontinuous on E. \] \[ x_0 E\; ε_0>0\; >0\; x E: (|x-x_0| \[ x_0 E\; (x_n)⊆ E: x_n→ x_0 and f(x_n) → f(x_0). \] \[ x_0 E\; V a neighborhood of f(x_0)\; U a neighborhood of x_0\; x U E: f(x) V. \]
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: This is the Lemma: \hyperref[prf:discontinuity-on-a-set] {Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere}{Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere}
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: This is the Lemma : {Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere}{Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere}

Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: For \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) and \(x_0\in E\), the epsilon-delta, sequential, and neighborhood characterizations of discontinuity at \(x_0\) are equivalent.

Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: 1. Prove epsilon-delta discontinuity implies sequential discontinuity by choosing x_n with delta=1/n. 2. Prove sequential discontinuity implies epsilon-delta discontinuity by using the negation of convergence of f(x_n). 3. Rewrite the epsilon-delta condition in neighborhood language using open intervals around x_0 and f(x_0).

Rewrite the epsilon-delta condition in neighborhood language using open intervals around x_0 and f(x_0).

Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: What is the image of a closed bounded interval under a continuous function?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What is the image of a closed bounded interval under a continuous function?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous, then \(f([a,b])\) is a closed bounded interval.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: If \(f:[a,b]→ℝ\) is continuous, then \(f([a,b])\) is a closed bounded interval.
Analysis · Continuity.hint: Boundedness gives bounded, extrema give endpoints, and IVT removes gaps.

Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Let \(I=[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}\), and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
f(I) \text{ is a closed bounded interval.}
\]

Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: \hyperref[prf:ivt-between-inf-and-sup] {Every Value Between \(\inf f(I)\) and \(\sup f(I)\) Is Attained}{Every Value Between inf f(I) and sup f(I) Is Attained}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
EVT gives $x_m, x_M$ with $f(x_m)=m$, $f(x_M)=M$. Set
$g = f-k$; continuous on $J = [\min(x_m,x_M), \max(x_m,x_M)]$.
$g(x_m) = m-k \leq 0$, $g(x_M) = M-k \geq 0$. Three cases: $k=m$
gives $c=x_m$; $k=M$ gives $c=x_M$; $m < k < M$ gives strict
inequalities so Location of Roots applies on $J$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: EVT gives x_m, x_M with f(x_m)=m, f(x_M)=M. Set g = f-k; continuous on J = [ (x_m,x_M), (x_m,x_M)]. g(x_m) = m-k ≤ 0, g(x_M) = M-k ≥ 0. Three cases: k=m gives c=x_m; k=M gives c=x_M; m < k < M gives strict inequalities so Location of Roots applies on J.

Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: What does a sign change force for a continuous function on a closed interval?

Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous and \(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\) have opposite signs, then there exists \(c\in(a,b)\) such that \(f(c)=0\).
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: If \(f:[a,b]→ℝ\) is continuous and \(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\) have opposite signs, then there exists \(c (a,b)\) such that \(f(c)=0\).
Analysis · Continuity.hint: A continuous path from negative to positive cannot jump over zero.

\ \wedge\
\bigl(f(a)<0<f(b)\ \vee\ f(a)>0>f(b)\bigr)
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in(a,b)\text{ such that } f(c)=0.
\]

Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: What does continuity on a closed bounded interval guarantee about extrema?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What does continuity on a closed bounded interval guarantee about extrema?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) has an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on \([a,b]\).
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: If \(f:[a,b]→ℝ\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) has an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on \([a,b]\).
Analysis · Continuity.hint: Continuity on a compact interval forces the extreme values not only to exist as bounds but to be attained.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:maximum-minimum-theorem
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:maximum-minimum-theorem]
{Maximum--Minimum Theorem}}{Maximum-Minimum Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:maximum-minimum-theorem] {Maximum--Minimum Theorem}{Maximum-Minimum Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
For all \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Bigl(
\exists x^{*}\in[a,b]\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ f(x^{*})\ge f(x)
\Bigr)
\]
and
\[
\Bigl(
\exists x_{*}\in[a,b]\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ f(x_{*})\le f(x)
\Bigr).
\]
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: For all \(a,b ℝ\) with \(a b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]→ℝ\), \[ f continuous on [a,b] ( x^{*} \; x ,\ f(x^{*}) f(x) ) \] and \[ ( x_{*} \; x ,\ f(x_{*}) f(x) ). \]
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: This is the Theorem: \hyperref[prf:maximum-minimum-theorem] {Maximum--Minimum Theorem}{Maximum-Minimum Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : {Maximum--Minimum Theorem}{Maximum-Minimum Theorem}

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:maximum-minimum-theorem
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:maximum-minimum-theorem]
{Maximum--Minimum Theorem}}{Maximum-Minimum Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:maximum-minimum-theorem] {Maximum--Minimum Theorem}{Maximum-Minimum Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Sketch the proof of: \texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:maximum-minimum-theorem]
{Maximum--Minimum Theorem}}{Maximum-Minimum Theorem}.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: \hyperref[prf:maximum-minimum-theorem] {Maximum--Minimum Theorem}{Maximum-Minimum Theorem}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: 1. Use boundedness to define sup f(I) and inf f(I). 2. Choose sequences whose function values approach these extrema. 3. Extract convergent subsequences in [a,b] and apply continuity to show the extreme values are attained.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: 1. Use boundedness to define sup f(I) and inf f(I). 2. Choose sequences whose function values approach these extrema. 3. Extract convergent subsequences in [a,b] and apply continuity to show the extreme values are attained.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:preservation-of-intervals
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:preservation-of-intervals]
{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}}{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:preservation-of-intervals] {Preservation of Intervals Theorem}{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: What does continuity do to intervals?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What does continuity do to intervals?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: A continuous image of an interval is an interval.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: A continuous image of an interval is an interval.
Analysis · Continuity.hint: This is the conceptual endpoint of IVT: continuous functions do not create gaps in images of intervals.

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:preservation-of-intervals
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:preservation-of-intervals]
{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}}{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:preservation-of-intervals] {Preservation of Intervals Theorem}{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Let \(I\subseteq\mathbb{R}\) be an interval and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
f(I) \text{ is an interval.}
\]
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Let \(I⊆ℝ\) be an interval and let \(f:I→ℝ\). Then \[ f continuous on I f(I) is an interval. \]
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex: This is the Theorem: \hyperref[prf:preservation-of-intervals] {Preservation of Intervals Theorem}{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : {Preservation of Intervals Theorem}{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}

Analysis · Continuity.label: thm:preservation-of-intervals
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex:
\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:preservation-of-intervals]
{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}}{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: \hyperref[prf:preservation-of-intervals] {Preservation of Intervals Theorem}{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
Sketch the proof of: \texorpdfstring{\hyperref[prf:preservation-of-intervals]
{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}}{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: \hyperref[prf:preservation-of-intervals] {Preservation of Intervals Theorem}{Preservation of Intervals Theorem}.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
Take $y_1, y_2 \in f(I)$, $y_1 < k < y_2$. Pull back:
$f(x_1)=y_1$, $f(x_2)=y_2$. $J = [\min(x_1,x_2),\max(x_1,x_2)]
\subseteq I$ since $I$ is an interval. IVT on $J$ gives $c$ with
$f(c)=k$. So $k \in f(I)$. Hence $f(I)$ is closed under betweenness,
i.e., an interval.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: Take y_1, y_2 f(I), y_1 < k < y_2. Pull back: f(x_1)=y_1, f(x_2)=y_2. J = [ (x_1,x_2), (x_1,x_2)] ⊆ I since I is an interval. IVT on J gives c with f(c)=k. So k f(I). Hence f(I) is closed under betweenness, i.e., an interval.

Analysis · Continuity.label: def:bi-lipschitz
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:bi-lipschitz
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:bi-lipschitz
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: What is a bi-Lipschitz map?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What is a bi-Lipschitz map?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
A function $f$ satisfying $\alpha|x-u| \leq |f(x)-f(u)|
  \leq K|x-u|$ for constants $0 < \alpha \leq K$. Both an upper
  and a lower Lipschitz bound.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: A function f satisfying |x-u| ≤ |f(x)-f(u)| ≤ K|x-u| for constants 0 < ≤ K. Both an upper and a lower Lipschitz bound.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Upper bound = Lipschitz. Lower bound = co-Lipschitz
  (expanding). Together they bound the metric distortion in both
  directions.

Analysis · Continuity.label: def:bi-lipschitz
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:bi-lipschitz
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:bi-lipschitz
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: Give a failure case or negation for: def:bi-lipschitz.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Give a failure case or negation for: def:bi-lipschitz.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f(x) = x^3$ on $[-1,1]$: Lipschitz ($|f'| \leq 3$) but
  not bi-Lipschitz since $f'(0) = 0$ — the lower bound fails
  near $0$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: f(x) = x^3 on [-1,1]: Lipschitz (|f'| ≤ 3) but not bi-Lipschitz since f'(0) = 0 — the lower bound fails near 0.

Analysis · Continuity.label: def:continuous-at-point
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:continuous-at-point
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:continuous-at-point
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
State the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition of continuity
  of $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ at $c \in A$.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: State the ε- definition of continuity of f : A → ℝ at c A.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$\forall\varepsilon>0,\,\exists\delta>0,\,\forall x\in A:\,
  |x-c|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon$.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Three differences from the limit definition: (1) no
  $0 <$ — $x=c$ is permitted; (2) target is $f(c)$, not a
  free $L$; (3) $c$ must lie in $A$.

Analysis · Continuity.label: def:continuous-at-point
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:continuous-at-point
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:continuous-at-point
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: Give a failure case or negation for: def:continuous-at-point.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Give a failure case or negation for: def:continuous-at-point.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f(x) = \sin(1/x)$ with $f(0) := 0$ is discontinuous
  at $0$: $\sin(1/x)$ oscillates between $\pm 1$ in every
  neighbourhood of $0$, so no $\delta$ works for
  $\varepsilon < 1$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: f(x) = (1/x) with f(0) := 0 is discontinuous at 0: (1/x) oscillates between 1 in every neighbourhood of 0, so no works for ε < 1.

Analysis · Continuity.label: def:continuous-at-point-nbhd
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:continuous-at-point-nbhd
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:continuous-at-point-nbhd
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
State the neighbourhood form of continuity of $f$
  at $c \in A$.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: State the neighbourhood form of continuity of f at c A.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
For every $V_\varepsilon(f(c))$ there exists
  $V_\delta(c)$ such that $f(A \cap V_\delta(c)) \subseteq
  V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: For every V_ε(f(c)) there exists V_ (c) such that f(A V_ (c)) ⊆ V_ε(f(c)).
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Same structure as the limit neighbourhood form but
  using the full neighbourhood $V_\delta(c)$, not the punctured
  $V_\delta^*(c)$.

Analysis · Continuity.label: def:continuous-at-point-seq
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:continuous-at-point-seq
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:continuous-at-point-seq
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex:
State the sequential definition of continuity of $f$
  at $c \in A$.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: State the sequential definition of continuity of f at c A.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
For every $(x_n) \subseteq A$ with $x_n \to c$,
  $f(x_n) \to f(c)$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: For every (x_n) ⊆ A with x_n → c, f(x_n) → f(c).
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
No $x_n \neq c$ condition — sequences may hit $c$.
  Contrast with the sequential criterion for limits where
  $x_n \neq c$ was required.

  = f(0)$. Discontinuous at $0$.

Analysis · Continuity.label: def:discontinuity-of-first-kind
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:discontinuity-of-first-kind
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:discontinuity-of-first-kind
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: What is a discontinuity of first kind?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What is a discontinuity of first kind?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
A discontinuity at $a$ where both one-sided limits exist, but
  at least one does not equal $f(a)$. Includes both removable and
  jump discontinuities.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: A discontinuity at a where both one-sided limits exist, but at least one does not equal f(a). Includes both removable and jump discontinuities.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
First kind = both one-sided limits exist. Second kind =
  at least one does not.

Analysis · Continuity.label: def:discontinuity-of-second-kind
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:discontinuity-of-second-kind
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:discontinuity-of-second-kind
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: What is a discontinuity of second kind?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What is a discontinuity of second kind?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
A discontinuity where at least one one-sided limit fails to
  exist. All discontinuities not of first kind are of second kind.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: A discontinuity where at least one one-sided limit fails to exist. All discontinuities not of first kind are of second kind.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Second kind = at least one one-sided limit missing.
  Infinite blow-up and oscillation are both second kind.

Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:equivalent-sequences
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:equivalent-sequences
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: Define equivalent sequences.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Define equivalent sequences.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$(a_n) \sim (b_n)$ iff $\lim_{n \to \infty}(a_n - b_n) = 0$,
  i.e., they eventually become arbitrarily close to each other.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: (a_n) (b_n) iff _{n → ∞}(a_n - b_n) = 0, i.e., they eventually become arbitrarily close to each other.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Convergence of $a_n \to L$ is the special case where
  $(b_n) = (L)$ is constant.

Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:infinite-adherent-points
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:infinite-adherent-points
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: When is $+\infty$ adherent to a set $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: When is +∞ adherent to a set X ⊆ ℝ?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
When $X$ is unbounded above, i.e., $\forall M \in \mathbb{R}$,
  $\exists x \in X$ with $x > M$, equivalently $\sup X = +\infty$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: When X is unbounded above, i.e., M ℝ, x X with x > M, equivalently X = +∞.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
The infinite analogue of: $x$ is adherent to $X$ iff $X$
  has elements arbitrarily close to $x$.

Analysis · Continuity.label: def:jump-of-function
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:jump-of-function
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:jump-of-function
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: Define the jump of an increasing function $f$ at $c$.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Define the jump of an increasing function f at c.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$j_f(c) = \lim_{x \to c^+} f(x) - \lim_{x \to c^-} f(x)$.
  Continuity at $c$ iff $j_f(c) = 0$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: j_f(c) = _{x → c^+} f(x) - _{x → c^-} f(x). Continuity at c iff j_f(c) = 0.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
The jump measures the gap in the graph at $c$. Positive
  jump = jump discontinuity; zero jump = continuous.

Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: Define $\lim_{x \to +\infty} f(x) = L$.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Define _{x → +∞} f(x) = L.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$\forall\varepsilon > 0,\;\exists M \in \mathbb{R}:\;
  x \in X,\, x > M \Rightarrow |f(x)-L| < \varepsilon$.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:

  $\varepsilon$ condition on the output.

Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f(x) = \sin x$: $\lim_{x \to +\infty} \sin x$ does not
  exist since $\sin x$ oscillates between $-1$ and $1$ for all large
  $x$.

Analysis · Continuity.label: def:limsup-liminf-function
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:limsup-liminf-function
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:limsup-liminf-function
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: Define $\limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x)$.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Define _{x → x_0} f(x).
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$\inf_{\delta > 0} \sup\{f(x) : x \in E,\; 0 < |x-x_0| <
  \delta\}$. The infimum over shrinking neighbourhoods of the
  supremum of $f$ values nearby.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: _{ > 0} \{f(x) : x E,\; 0 < |x-x_0| < \}. The infimum over shrinking neighbourhoods of the supremum of f values nearby.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Shrink the neighbourhood; the sup of $f$ on it can only
  decrease; the limit of that decreasing function is the $\limsup$.

Analysis · Continuity.label: def:lipschitz-condition
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:lipschitz-condition
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:lipschitz-condition
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: Define a Lipschitz function $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Define a Lipschitz function f : A → ℝ.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$f$ is Lipschitz if $\exists K > 0$ such that
  $|f(x)-f(u)| \leq K|x-u|$ for all $x,u \in A$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: f is Lipschitz if K > 0 such that |f(x)-f(u)| ≤ K|x-u| for all x,u A.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
The secant slope interpretation: all chords on the graph
  have slope $\leq K$ in absolute value.

$\sqrt{x}$ on $[0,1]$: secant slopes through $(0,0)$ are
  $1/\sqrt{x} \to \infty$ as $x \to 0^+$, so no $K$ works.

Analysis · Continuity.label: def:point-of-discontinuity
Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:point-of-discontinuity
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:point-of-discontinuity
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: What is a point of discontinuity?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What is a point of discontinuity?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
A point $a \in E$ where $f$ is not continuous. Equivalently,
  $\exists\varepsilon_0>0$ such that no $\delta>0$ keeps $f$ within
  $\varepsilon_0$ of $f(a)$ on all of $U_\delta(a) \cap E$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: A point a E where f is not continuous. Equivalently, ε_0>0 such that no >0 keeps f within ε_0 of f(a) on all of U_ (a) E.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
The negation of continuity at $a$ — a fixed output gap
  that cannot be eliminated by shrinking the input window.

Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:relative-neighborhood
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:relative-neighborhood
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: Define the relative neighborhood of $a$ in $E$.
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: Define the relative neighborhood of a in E.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
$U_E(a) = E \cap U(a)$ where $U(a)$ is a neighborhood of $a$
  in $\mathbb{R}$. It is the part of $E$ within a full neighborhood
  of $a$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: U_E(a) = E U(a) where U(a) is a neighborhood of a in ℝ. It is the part of E within a full neighborhood of a.
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
Relativizing: intersect the usual ball with the domain $E$.
  Used to state local properties precisely on arbitrary subsets.

Analysis · Continuity.name_latex: def:removable-discontinuity
Analysis · Continuity.name_plain: def:removable-discontinuity
Analysis · Continuity.question_latex: What is a removable discontinuity?
Analysis · Continuity.question_plain: What is a removable discontinuity?
Analysis · Continuity.answer_latex:
A discontinuity at $a$ that can be repaired by redefining
  $f$ at $a$ alone. The two-sided limit exists but does not equal
  $f(a)$.
Analysis · Continuity.answer_plain: A discontinuity at a that can be repaired by redefining f at a alone. The two-sided limit exists but does not equal f(a).
Analysis · Continuity.hint:
The graph has the right limiting behavior but the wrong
  point value. One point can fix it.
