Limits.label: thm:limit-unique
Limits.question_latex: Why can a function have at most one limit at a cluster point?
Limits.question_plain: Why can a function have at most one limit at a cluster point?
Limits.answer_latex:
Assume $\lim f = L$ and $\lim f = L'$. Set
  $\varepsilon = |L-L'|/2$. Invoke each limit definition to get
  $\delta_1$, $\delta_2$; take $\delta = \min(\delta_1,\delta_2)$.
  Triangle inequality gives $|L-L'| < |L-L'|$, contradiction.
Limits.answer_plain: Assume f = L and f = L'. Set ε = |L-L'|/2. Invoke each limit definition to get _1, _2; take = ( _1, _2). Triangle inequality gives |L-L'| < |L-L'|, contradiction.
Limits.hint:
The key pattern: invoke the limit definition twice,
  once for each candidate, then reconcile with
  $\delta=\min(\delta_1,\delta_2)$.

Limits.label: thm:limit-unique
Limits.question_latex:
\[
  \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L
  \;\wedge\;
  \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L'
  \;\Rightarrow\; L = L'.
\]
Limits.answer_latex: This is the Theorem: Uniqueness of Limits of Functions
Limits.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : Uniqueness of Limits of Functions

Limits.question_latex: Give a failure case or negation for: Uniqueness of Limits of Functions.
Limits.question_plain: Give a failure case or negation for: Uniqueness of Limits of Functions.
Limits.answer_latex:
Without the cluster point condition the limit can be
  vacuously satisfied by every $L$ (isolated point).
Limits.answer_plain: Without the cluster point condition the limit can be vacuously satisfied by every L (isolated point).

Limits.label: thm:limit-unique
Limits.question_latex: Sketch the proof of: Uniqueness of Limits of Functions.
Limits.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: Uniqueness of Limits of Functions.
Limits.answer_latex:
1. Assume $L \neq L'$; set $\varepsilon = |L-L'|/2 > 0$.
2. Invoke limit definition twice to get $\delta_1$, $\delta_2$; set
   $\delta = \min(\delta_1, \delta_2)$.
3. Find $x \in A$ with $0 < |x-c| < \delta$; triangle inequality gives
   $|L-L'| < |L-L'|$, contradiction.
Limits.answer_plain: 1. Assume L ≠ L'; set ε = |L-L'|/2 > 0. 2. Invoke limit definition twice to get _1, _2; set = ( _1, _2). 3. Find x A with 0 < |x-c| < ; triangle inequality gives |L-L'| < |L-L'|, contradiction.

Limits.label: def:van-der-waerden
Limits.question_latex: State the definition: Van der Waerden function.
Limits.question_plain: State the definition: Van der Waerden function.
Limits.answer_latex:
\cite[Theorem~8.3.8]{SohrabBasicRealAnalysis2}
Let $f_0(x) := d(x, \mathbb{Z}) = \min_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}|x-n|$
be the distance from $x$ to the nearest integer (a continuous sawtooth
with period $1$ and amplitude $1/2$). Define
$f_n(x) := 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x)$ for $n \geq 0$. The
\emph{Van der Waerden function} is
\[
  W(x) := \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} f_n(x)
         = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x).
\]
Limits.answer_plain: SohrabBasicRealAnalysis2 Let f_0(x) := d(x, Z) = _{n Z}|x-n| be the distance from x to the nearest integer (a continuous sawtooth with period 1 and amplitude 1/2). Define f_n(x) := 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x) for n ≥ 0. The Van der Waerden function is \[ W(x) := _{n=0}^{∞} f_n(x) = _{n=0}^{∞} 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x). \]

Limits.label: def:vertical-asymptote
Limits.question_latex: Define vertical asymptote.
Limits.question_plain: Define vertical asymptote.
Limits.answer_latex:
$x = x_0$ is a vertical asymptote if any one of
$\lim_{x\to x_0}f(x)$, $\lim_{x\to x_0^\pm}f(x)$ is $\pm\infty$.
Limits.answer_plain: x = x_0 is a vertical asymptote if any one of _{x→ x_0}f(x), _{x→ x_0^ }f(x) is ∞.
Limits.hint:
Vertical asymptotes describe blow-up at a finite input.
Only one one-sided limit needs to be infinite.

Limits.label: def:vertical-asymptote
Limits.question_latex:
$x = x_0$ is a vertical asymptote $\iff$
at least one of $\lim_{x\to x_0} f(x)$,
$\lim_{x\to x_0^-} f(x)$, $\lim_{x\to x_0^+} f(x)$
is $\pm\infty$.
Limits.question_plain: x = x_0 is a vertical asymptote iff at least one of _{x→ x_0} f(x), _{x→ x_0^-} f(x), _{x→ x_0^+} f(x) is ∞.
Limits.answer_latex: This is the Definition: Vertical Asymptote
Limits.answer_plain: This is the Definition : Vertical Asymptote

Limits.label: def:vertical-asymptote

Logarithmic.answer_latex:
$x^r$ dominates: $(\ln x)/x^r \to 0$ as $x \to \infty$;
$x^r \ln x \to 0$ as $x \to 0^+$ (even though $\ln x \to -\infty$).

Multiplication.label: def:exp
Multiplication.answer_latex:
Let $m \in \mathbb{N}$. Exponentiation is defined recursively:
\[
m^0 := 1, \qquad m^{n\pp} := m^n \times m.
\]
In particular, $0^0 := 1$.
Multiplication.answer_plain: Let m ℕ. Exponentiation is defined recursively: \[ m^0 := 1, m^{n } := m^n m. \] In particular, 0^0 := 1.

Multiplication.label: lem:mult-comm
Multiplication.answer_latex: For all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $n \times m = m \times n$.
Multiplication.answer_plain: For all n, m ℕ, n m = m n.

Multiplication.label: lem:no-zero-div
Multiplication.question_latex: State the lemma: Tao 2.3.3 — No zero divisors.
Multiplication.question_plain: State the lemma: Tao 2.3.3 — No zero divisors.
Multiplication.answer_latex:
For all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $n \times m = 0$ if and only if
$n = 0$ or $m = 0$.

In particular, if $n$ and $m$ are both positive, then $nm$ is positive.
Multiplication.answer_plain: For all n, m ℕ, n m = 0 if and only if n = 0 or m = 0. In particular, if n and m are both positive, then nm is positive.

Multiplication.label: prop:distrib
Multiplication.question_latex: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.4 — Distributive law.
Multiplication.question_plain: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.4 — Distributive law.
Multiplication.answer_latex:
For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$:
\[
a(b+c) = ab + ac \qquad \text{and} \qquad (b+c)a = ba + ca.
\]
Multiplication.answer_plain: For all a, b, c ℕ: \[ a(b+c) = ab + ac and (b+c)a = ba + ca. \]

Multiplication.label: prop:mult-assoc
Multiplication.answer_latex: For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $(a \times b) \times c = a \times (b \times c)$.
Multiplication.answer_plain: For all a, b, c ℕ, (a b) c = a (b c).

Multiplication.label: prop:mult-order
Multiplication.question_latex: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.6 — Multiplication preserves order.
Multiplication.question_plain: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.6 — Multiplication preserves order.
Multiplication.answer_latex: If $a < b$ and $c$ is positive, then $ac < bc$.
Multiplication.answer_plain: If a < b and c is positive, then ac < bc.

Multiplication.label: cor:mult-cancel
Multiplication.question_latex: State the corollary: Tao 2.3.7 — Cancellation for multiplication.
Multiplication.question_plain: State the corollary: Tao 2.3.7 — Cancellation for multiplication.
Multiplication.answer_latex: Let $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$ with $ac = bc$ and $c \neq 0$. Then $a = b$.
Multiplication.answer_plain: Let a, b, c ℕ with ac = bc and c ≠ 0. Then a = b.

Multiplication.label: prop:euclid
Multiplication.question_latex: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.9 — Euclidean algorithm.
Multiplication.question_plain: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.9 — Euclidean algorithm.
Multiplication.answer_latex:
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$ and let $q$ be positive. Then there exist
$m, r \in \mathbb{N}$ such that:
\[
0 \leq r < q \qquad \text{and} \qquad n = mq + r.
\]
Multiplication.answer_plain: Let n ℕ and let q be positive. Then there exist m, r ℕ such that: \[ 0 ≤ r < q and n = mq + r. \]

Ordinary Differential Equations.label: def:negation-continuity-at-a-point
Ordinary Differential Equations.name_latex: Negation of Continuity at a Point
Ordinary Differential Equations.name_plain: Negation of Continuity at a Point
Ordinary Differential Equations.question_latex: What is the negation of continuity at a point?
Ordinary Differential Equations.question_plain: What is the negation of continuity at a point?
Ordinary Differential Equations.answer_latex:
A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is not continuous at \(x_0\in E\) if and only if there exists \(\varepsilon_0>0\) such that for every \(\delta>0\), there exists \(x\in E\) with
\[
|x-x_0|<\delta
\quad\text{and}\quad
|f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0.
\]
Ordinary Differential Equations.answer_plain: A function \(f:E→ℝ\) is not continuous at \(x_0 E\) if and only if there exists \(ε_0>0\) such that for every \( >0\), there exists \(x E\) with \[ |x-x_0|< and |f(x)-f(x_0)| ε_0. \]
Ordinary Differential Equations.hint: Negate the quantifiers in order: \(\forall\exists\forall\) becomes \(\exists\forall\exists\), and the conclusion \(<\varepsilon\) becomes \(\ge \varepsilon\).

Ordinary Differential Equations.label: def:negation-continuity-at-a-point
Ordinary Differential Equations.name_latex: Negation of Continuity at a Point
Ordinary Differential Equations.name_plain: Negation of Continuity at a Point
Ordinary Differential Equations.question_latex:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0\in E\). Then
\[
f \text{ is not continuous at } x_0
\]
if and only if
\[
\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E:
\bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr).
\]
Ordinary Differential Equations.question_plain: Let \(E ⊆ ℝ\), let \(f : E → ℝ\), and let \(x_0 E\). Then \[ f is not continuous at x_0 \] if and only if \[ ε_0>0\; >0\; x E: (|x-x_0|< \ \ |f(x)-f(x_0)| ε_0 ). \]
Ordinary Differential Equations.answer_latex: This is the Definition: Negation of Continuity at a Point
Ordinary Differential Equations.answer_plain: This is the Definition : Negation of Continuity at a Point

Predicate Logic · Quantifiers.label: thm:qcomm
Predicate Logic · Quantifiers.answer_latex:
Quantifiers of the same type commute:
\begin{align*}
\forall x\,\forall y\,\varphi &\;\equiv\; \forall y\,\forall x\,\varphi, \\
\exists x\,\exists y\,\varphi &\;\equiv\; \exists y\,\exists x\,\varphi.
\end{align*}
However, quantifiers of different types do \emph{not} commute in general:
\[
\forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi \;\not\equiv\; \exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi.
\]
Predicate Logic · Quantifiers.answer_plain: Quantifiers of the same type commute: align* x\, y\, &\; \; y\, x\, , \\ x\, y\, &\; \; y\, x\, . align* However, quantifiers of different types do not commute in general: \[ x\, y\, \; \; y\, x\, . \]

Predicate Logic · Semantics.question_latex: State the lemma: Substitution Lemma for Formulas.
Predicate Logic · Semantics.question_plain: State the lemma: Substitution Lemma for Formulas.
Predicate Logic · Semantics.answer_latex:
Let $\varphi$ be a formula, $t$ a term free for $x$ in $\varphi$. Then for
any structure $\mathcal{M}$ and assignment $s$,
\[
\mathcal{M},s \models \varphi[t/x]
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal{M},\,s\bigl[x\mapsto \llbracket t \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},s}\bigr] \models \varphi.
\]
Predicate Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: Let be a formula, t a term free for x in . Then for any structure M and assignment s, \[ M,s M,\,s . \]

Predicate Logic · Semantics.label: lem:subst-terms
Predicate Logic · Semantics.question_latex: State the lemma: Substitution Lemma for Terms.
Predicate Logic · Semantics.question_plain: State the lemma: Substitution Lemma for Terms.
Predicate Logic · Semantics.answer_latex:
Let $\mathcal{M}$ be a structure, $s$ a variable assignment, $x$ a variable,
and $d \in D$. For any term $t$,
\[
\llbracket t \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},\,s[x\mapsto d]}
=
\llbracket t[d/x] \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},\,s}.
\]
Predicate Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: Let M be a structure, s a variable assignment, x a variable, and d D. For any term t, \[ t _{M,\,s[x d]} = t[d/x] _{M,\,s}. \]

Proof Techniques.label: prop:bw-bisection
Proof Techniques.answer_latex: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence.
Proof Techniques.answer_plain: Every bounded sequence in ℝ has a convergent subsequence.
Proof Techniques.hint: Extract a monotone subsequence; bounded monotone sequences converge by MCT.

Proof Techniques.label: prop:convergent-bounded
Proof Techniques.answer_latex: If $(b_n)\to b$ then $\exists M>0$ with $|b_n|\le M$ for all $n$.
Proof Techniques.answer_plain: If (b_n)→ b then M>0 with |b_n| M for all n.
Proof Techniques.hint: Take $\varepsilon=1$: beyond $N$ all terms lie in $(b-1,b+1)$; finitely many terms before $N$ give a finite bound.

Proof Techniques.label: prop:ivt-bisection
Proof Techniques.answer_latex: If $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $f(a)<L<f(b)$ (or $f(a)>L>f(b)$), then $\exists c\in(a,b): f(c)=L$.
Proof Techniques.answer_plain: If f:[a,b]→ℝ is continuous and f(a) L>f(b)), then c (a,b): f(c)=L.
Proof Techniques.hint: Bisection: maintain $f(a_n)<L<f(b_n)$; the intersection point $c$ satisfies $f(c)=L$ by continuity.

Proof Techniques.label: prop:monotone-approx-bounds
Proof Techniques.question_latex: State Monotone Approximation of Bounds (proof-techniques version).
Proof Techniques.question_plain: State Monotone Approximation of Bounds (proof-techniques version).
Proof Techniques.answer_latex: For nonempty bounded $S$: there exist monotone sequences in $S$ converging to $\sup S$ and $\inf S$.
Proof Techniques.answer_plain: For nonempty bounded S: there exist monotone sequences in S converging to S and S.

Proof Techniques.label: prop:order-arithmetic
Proof Techniques.question_latex: State the order arithmetic properties of $\mathbb{R}$.
Proof Techniques.question_plain: State the order arithmetic properties of ℝ.
Proof Techniques.answer_latex: (i) $a\le b,c\le d\Rightarrow a+c\le b+d$; (ii) $a\le b,c>0\Rightarrow ac\le bc$; (iii) $a\le b,c<0\Rightarrow ac\ge bc$.
Proof Techniques.answer_plain: (i) a b,c d⇒ a+c b+d; (ii) a b,c>0⇒ ac bc; (iii) a b,c<0⇒ ac bc.
Proof Techniques.hint: Multiplication by a negative flips the inequality.

Proof Techniques.label: prop:residue-divergence
Proof Techniques.answer_latex: If residue subsequences $(a_{kn+r})\to L$ and $(a_{kn+s})\to M$ with $L\neq M$, then $(a_n)$ diverges.
Proof Techniques.answer_plain: If residue subsequences (a_{kn+r})→ L and (a_{kn+s})→ M with L≠ M, then (a_n) diverges.

Proof Techniques.label: prop:io-ev-dichotomy
Proof Techniques.answer_latex: For any predicate $P$: exactly one of ($P$ infinitely often) or ($\neg P$ eventually) holds.
Proof Techniques.answer_plain: For any predicate P: exactly one of (P infinitely often) or ( P eventually) holds.

Proof Techniques.label: prop:r-uncountable
Proof Techniques.answer_latex: $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable: any proposed enumeration can be contradicted by a nested-interval construction.
Proof Techniques.answer_plain: ℝ is uncountable: any proposed enumeration can be contradicted by a nested-interval construction.

Propositional Logic · Metatheory.label: cor:consequence-compact
Propositional Logic · Metatheory.question_latex: State the corollary: Compactness of Logical Consequence.
Propositional Logic · Metatheory.question_plain: State the corollary: Compactness of Logical Consequence.
Propositional Logic · Metatheory.answer_latex:
If $\Gamma \models \varphi$, then there exists a \emph{finite} subset
$\Gamma_0 \subseteq \Gamma$ with $\Gamma_0 \models \varphi$.

Equivalently: logical consequence from an infinite premise set always depends
on only finitely many premises.
Propositional Logic · Metatheory.answer_plain: If , then there exists a finite subset _0 ⊆ with _0 . Equivalently: logical consequence from an infinite premise set always depends on only finitely many premises.

Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.label: def:bicond-e
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_plain: \[ P \leftrightarrow Q{P Q} P \leftrightarrow Q{Q P} \]

Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.label: def:cond-i
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.question_latex: State the definition: Conditional Introduction (Conditional Proof).
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.question_plain: State the definition: Conditional Introduction (Conditional Proof).
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_latex:
If assuming $P$ in a subproof leads to $Q$, then discharge the assumption and
infer $P \rightarrow Q$:
\[
\frac{[P \vdash Q]}{P \rightarrow Q}
\]
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_plain: If assuming P in a subproof leads to Q, then discharge the assumption and infer P Q: \[ [P \vdash Q]{P Q} \]

Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.label: def:conj-e
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_latex:
From a conjunction, infer either conjunct:
\[
\frac{P \wedge Q}{P} \qquad \frac{P \wedge Q}{Q}
\]
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_plain: From a conjunction, infer either conjunct: \[ P \wedge Q{P} P \wedge Q{Q} \]

Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.label: def:conj-i
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_latex:
From two formulas, infer their conjunction:
\[
\frac{P \qquad Q}{P \wedge Q}
\]
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_plain: From two formulas, infer their conjunction: \[ P \qquad Q{P Q} \]

Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.label: def:cd
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_plain: \[ P \vee Q \qquad P \rightarrow R \qquad Q \rightarrow S{R S} \]

Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.label: def:dd
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_latex:
\[
 rac{P ightarrow R \qquad Q ightarrow S \qquad 
eg R \vee 
eg S}{
eg P \vee 
eg Q}
\]
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_plain: \[ P \rightarrow R \qquad Q \rightarrow S \qquad \neg R \vee \neg S{ P Q} \]

Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.label: def:disj-e
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.question_latex: State the definition: Disjunction Elimination (Proof by Cases).
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.question_plain: State the definition: Disjunction Elimination (Proof by Cases).
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_latex:
From a disjunction, if the same conclusion $R$ follows from each disjunct, infer $R$:
\[
\frac{P \vee Q \qquad [P \vdash R] \qquad [Q \vdash R]}{R}
\]
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_plain: From a disjunction, if the same conclusion R follows from each disjunct, infer R: \[ P \vee Q \qquad [P \vdash R] \qquad [Q \vdash R]{R} \]

Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.label: def:disj-i
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_latex:
From a proposition, infer any disjunction containing it:
\[
\frac{P}{P \vee Q}
\]
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_plain: From a proposition, infer any disjunction containing it: \[ P{P Q} \]

Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.label: def:dne
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.question_latex: State the definition: Double Negation Elimination.
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.question_plain: State the definition: Double Negation Elimination.

Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.label: def:hs
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_latex:
From two conditionals chained, infer the composed conditional:
\[
\frac{P \rightarrow Q \qquad Q \rightarrow R}{P \rightarrow R}
\]
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_plain: From two conditionals chained, infer the composed conditional: \[ P \rightarrow Q \qquad Q \rightarrow R{P R} \]

Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.label: def:mp
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_latex:
From a conditional and its antecedent, infer the consequent:
\[
\frac{P \rightarrow Q \qquad P}{Q}
\]
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_plain: From a conditional and its antecedent, infer the consequent: \[ P \rightarrow Q \qquad P{Q} \]

Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.label: def:mt
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_latex:
From a conditional and the negation of its consequent, infer the negation of
the antecedent:
\[
\frac{P \rightarrow Q \qquad \neg Q}{\neg P}
\]
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_plain: From a conditional and the negation of its consequent, infer the negation of the antecedent: \[ P \rightarrow Q \qquad \neg Q{ P} \]

Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.label: def:neg-e
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.question_latex: State the definition: Negation Elimination (Indirect Proof).
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.question_plain: State the definition: Negation Elimination (Indirect Proof).
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_latex:
If assuming $\neg P$ leads to $\bot$, infer $P$:
\[
\frac{[\neg P \vdash \bot]}{P}
\]
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_plain: If assuming P leads to , infer P: \[ [\neg P \vdash \bot]{P} \]

Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.label: def:neg-i
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.question_latex: State the definition: Negation Introduction (Reductio ad Absurdum).
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.question_plain: State the definition: Negation Introduction (Reductio ad Absurdum).
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_latex:
If assuming $P$ leads to contradiction $\bot$, infer $\neg P$:
\[
\frac{[P \vdash \bot]}{\neg P}
\]
Propositional Logic · Proof Theory.answer_plain: If assuming P leads to contradiction , infer P: \[ [P \vdash \bot]{ P} \]

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: eq:absorb
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex: $P \wedge (P \vee Q) \equiv P$;\qquad $P \vee (P \wedge Q) \equiv P$
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: P (P Q) P; P (P Q) P

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: eq:assoc
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex:
$(P \wedge Q) \wedge R \equiv P \wedge (Q \wedge R)$;\qquad
$(P \vee Q) \vee R \equiv P \vee (Q \vee R)$
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: (P Q) R P (Q R); (P Q) R P (Q R)

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: eq:bicond-exp
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex: $P \leftrightarrow Q \;\equiv\; (P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (Q \rightarrow P)$
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: P Q \; \; (P Q) (Q P)

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: eq:comm
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex: $P \wedge Q \equiv Q \wedge P$;\qquad $P \vee Q \equiv Q \vee P$
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: P Q Q P; P Q Q P

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: eq:contra
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex: $P \rightarrow Q \;\equiv\; \neg Q \rightarrow \neg P$
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: P Q \; \; Q P

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: eq:demorgan
Propositional Logic · Semantics.question_latex: State the proposition: De Morgan's Laws.
Propositional Logic · Semantics.question_plain: State the proposition: De Morgan's Laws.
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: align* (P Q) &\; \; P Q \\ (P Q) &\; \; P Q align*

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: eq:dist
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex:
$P \wedge (Q \vee R) \equiv (P \wedge Q) \vee (P \wedge R)$;\qquad
$P \vee (Q \wedge R) \equiv (P \vee Q) \wedge (P \vee R)$
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: P (Q R) (P Q) (P R); P (Q R) (P Q) (P R)

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: eq:domination
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex: $P \vee \top \equiv \top$;\qquad $P \wedge \bot \equiv \bot$
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: P →p →p; P

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: eq:double-neg
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex: $\neg\neg P \;\equiv\; P$

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: prop:cnf-exists
Propositional Logic · Semantics.question_latex: State the proposition: Every Formula Has a CNF.
Propositional Logic · Semantics.question_plain: State the proposition: Every Formula Has a CNF.
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex: Every propositional formula is logically equivalent to a formula in CNF.
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: Every propositional formula is logically equivalent to a formula in CNF.

Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: Every propositional formula is logically equivalent to a formula in DNF.

Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex: This is the Proposition: Every Formula Has a DNF

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: prop:nnf-exists
Propositional Logic · Semantics.question_latex: State the proposition: Every Formula Has an NNF.
Propositional Logic · Semantics.question_plain: State the proposition: Every Formula Has an NNF.

Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: Every propositional formula is logically equivalent to a formula in NNF.

Propositional Logic · Semantics.question_plain: :\; in NNF,\; (same truth value under every assignment v: Var→\{T,F\}).
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex: This is the Proposition: Every Formula Has an NNF
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: This is the Proposition : Every Formula Has an NNF

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: eq:export
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex: $(P \wedge Q) \rightarrow R \;\equiv\; P \rightarrow (Q \rightarrow R)$
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: (P Q) R \; \; P (Q R)

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: eq:idemp
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex: $P \wedge P \equiv P$;\qquad $P \vee P \equiv P$
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: P P P; P P P

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: eq:identity
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex: $P \wedge \top \equiv P$;\qquad $P \vee \bot \equiv P$
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: P →p P; P P

Propositional Logic · Semantics.label: eq:mat-impl
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_latex: $P \rightarrow Q \;\equiv\; \neg P \vee Q$
Propositional Logic · Semantics.answer_plain: P Q \; \; P Q

$P \vee \neg P \equiv \top$ \quad (Law of Excluded Middle);\qquad

Propositional Logic · Syntax.label: prop:nand-complete
Propositional Logic · Syntax.question_latex: State the proposition: Non-Associativity of NAND.
Propositional Logic · Syntax.question_plain: State the proposition: Non-Associativity of NAND.
Propositional Logic · Syntax.answer_latex: NAND is \textbf{not associative}: $(P \uparrow Q) \uparrow R \not\equiv P \uparrow (Q \uparrow R)$.
Propositional Logic · Syntax.answer_plain: NAND is not associative: (P Q) R P (Q R).

Propositional Logic · Syntax.label: prop:nor-complete
Propositional Logic · Syntax.question_latex: State the proposition: Non-Associativity of NOR.
Propositional Logic · Syntax.question_plain: State the proposition: Non-Associativity of NOR.
Propositional Logic · Syntax.answer_latex: NOR is \textbf{not associative}: $(P \downarrow Q) \downarrow R \not\equiv P \downarrow (Q \downarrow R)$.
Propositional Logic · Syntax.answer_plain: NOR is not associative: (P Q) R P (Q R).

Rationals.label: def:rational-operations
Rationals.question_latex: State the definition: Addition and multiplication.
Rationals.question_plain: State the definition: Addition and multiplication.
Rationals.answer_latex:
Let $[(a,b)],[(c,d)]\in\mathbb{Q}$, where $(a,b),(c,d)\in W$.  Define
\[
[(a,b)] + [(c,d)] := [(ad+bc,bd)]
\]
and
\[
[(a,b)]\cdot[(c,d)] := [(ac,bd)].
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: Let [(a,b)],[(c,d)] ℚ, where (a,b),(c,d) W. Define \[ [(a,b)] + [(c,d)] := [(ad+bc,bd)] \] and \[ [(a,b)] := [(ac,bd)]. \]

Rationals.label: def:archimedean
Rationals.question_plain: State the definition: Archimedean Property.
Rationals.answer_latex:
An ordered field $F$ is said to satisfy the \emph{Archimedean property}
if for every element $x \in F$ there exists a natural number $n$ such
that

\[
n > x.
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: An ordered field F is said to satisfy the Archimedean property if for every element x F there exists a natural number n such that \[ n > x. \]

Rationals.label: thm:archimedean-q
Rationals.question_latex: State the theorem: Archimedean property of $\mathbb{Q}$.
Rationals.question_plain: State the theorem: Archimedean property of ℚ.
Rationals.answer_latex:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
n>x.
\]
Equivalently, for every $x>0$ there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
\frac{1}{n}<x.
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: For every x ℚ there exists n ℕ such that \[ n>x. \] Equivalently, for every x>0 there exists n ℕ such that \[ 1{n}<x. \]

Rationals.label: def:cauchy-rational
Rationals.question_latex: State the definition: Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{Q}$.
Rationals.question_plain: State the definition: Cauchy sequence in ℚ.
Rationals.answer_latex:
A sequence $(x_n)$ of rational numbers is called a \emph{Cauchy sequence} if
\[
\forall \varepsilon>0\ \exists N\in\mathbb{N}\ \forall m,n\ge N,
\qquad
|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon.
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: A sequence (x_n) of rational numbers is called a Cauchy sequence if \[ ε>0\ N ℕ\ m,n N, |x_m-x_n|<ε. \]

Rationals.label: lem:sb-denom-growth
Rationals.name_latex: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths
Rationals.name_plain: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths
Rationals.question_latex: State the lemma: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths.
Rationals.question_plain: State the lemma: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths.
Rationals.answer_latex:
Let $a_0/b_0, a_1/b_1, a_2/b_2, \ldots$ be the sequence of fractions
encountered along any infinite path in the Stern--Brocot tree (i.e.\ the
sequence of interval-endpoint fractions at successive levels). Then
\[
  b_0 < b_1 < b_2 < \cdots,
\]
and in particular $b_n \to \infty$.
Rationals.answer_plain: Let a_0/b_0, a_1/b_1, a_2/b_2, be the sequence of fractions encountered along any infinite path in the Stern--Brocot tree (i.e.\ the sequence of interval-endpoint fractions at successive levels). Then \[ b_0 < b_1 < b_2 < , \] and in particular b_n → ∞.

Rationals.label: lem:sb-denom-growth
Rationals.name_latex: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths
Rationals.name_plain: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths
Rationals.question_latex:
$\forall n \in \mathbb{N},\ b_n < b_{n+1}$.
In particular, $\forall M > 0\ \exists N \in \mathbb{N}\ \forall n \ge N,\ b_n > M$.
Rationals.question_plain: n ℕ,\ b_n 0\ N ℕ\ n N,\ b_n > M.
Rationals.answer_latex: This is the Lemma: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths
Rationals.answer_plain: This is the Lemma : Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths

Rationals.label: thm:q-dense-in-r
Rationals.question_latex: State the theorem: Density of $\mathbb{Q}$ in $\mathbb{R}$.
Rationals.question_plain: State the theorem: Density of ℚ in ℝ.
Rationals.answer_latex:
If $x,y\in\mathbb{R}$ and $x<y$, then there exists $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
x<q<y.
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: If x,y ℝ and x<y, then there exists q ℚ such that \[ x<q<y. \]

Rationals.label: cor:q-dense-in-q
Rationals.question_latex: State the corollary: Density of $\mathbb{Q}$ within itself.
Rationals.question_plain: State the corollary: Density of ℚ within itself.
Rationals.answer_latex:
If $r,s\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $r<s$, then there exists $t\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
r<t<s.
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: If r,s ℚ with r<s, then there exists t ℚ such that \[ r<t<s. \]

Rationals.label: thm:dirichlet-approx
Rationals.question_latex: State the theorem: Dirichlet's Approximation Theorem.
Rationals.question_plain: State the theorem: Dirichlet's Approximation Theorem.
Rationals.answer_latex:
For every real number $x$ there exist infinitely many rational numbers $p/q$ with $p\in\mathbb{Z}$ and
$q\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
\left|x-\frac{p}{q}\right|<\frac{1}{q^2}.
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: For every real number x there exist infinitely many rational numbers p/q with p Z and q ℕ such that \[ |x-p{q} |<1{q^2}. \]

Rationals.label: def:rational-distinguished
Rationals.question_plain: State the definition: Distinguished elements.
Rationals.answer_latex:
Define
\[
0 := [(0,1)]
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
1 := [(1,1)].
\]

Rationals.label: def:fraction-pair-equality
Rationals.question_latex: State the definition: Equality of fraction pairs.
Rationals.question_plain: State the definition: Equality of fraction pairs.
Rationals.answer_latex:
For $(a,b),(c,d)\in W$, define
\[
(a,b) \sim (c,d)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
ad = bc.
\]

In fully quantified form,
\[
\forall (a,b),(c,d)\in W,
\qquad
(a,b)\sim(c,d)
\Longleftrightarrow
ad=bc.
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: For (a,b),(c,d) W, define \[ (a,b) (c,d) ad = bc. \] In fully quantified form, \[ (a,b),(c,d) W, (a,b) (c,d) ad=bc. \]

Rationals.label: cor:sb-path-limit
Rationals.question_latex: State the corollary: Every infinite Stern--Brocot path converges.
Rationals.question_plain: State the corollary: Every infinite Stern--Brocot path converges.
Rationals.answer_latex:
Let $x > 0$ be a real number and let $(I_n)$ be the Stern--Brocot
refinement sequence targeting $x$.  Then the sequence of left endpoints
$(a_n/b_n)$ and the sequence of right endpoints $(c_n/d_n)$ both
converge in $\mathbb{R}$ to a common limit $\xi$, and $\xi = x$.
Rationals.answer_plain: Let x > 0 be a real number and let (I_n) be the Stern--Brocot refinement sequence targeting x. Then the sequence of left endpoints (a_n/b_n) and the sequence of right endpoints (c_n/d_n) both converge in ℝ to a common limit , and = x.

Rationals.label: cor:sb-path-limit
Rationals.answer_latex: This is the Corollary: Every infinite Stern--Brocot path converges
Rationals.answer_plain: This is the Corollary : Every infinite Stern--Brocot path converges

Rationals.label: thm:farey-neighbor
Rationals.question_latex: State the theorem: Farey neighbor determinant condition.
Rationals.question_plain: State the theorem: Farey neighbor determinant condition.
Rationals.answer_latex:
Suppose
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\qquad\text{with}\qquad
\gcd(a,b)=\gcd(c,d)=1,
\quad b,d>0.
\]
If
\[
bc-ad=1,
\]
then the two fractions are neighbors in a Farey sequence, and their mediant is already in reduced
form:
\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}.
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: Suppose \[ a{b} 0. \] If \[ bc-ad=1, \] then the two fractions are neighbors in a Farey sequence, and their mediant is already in reduced form: \[ a+c{b+d}. \]

Rationals.label: def:fraction-pairs
Rationals.question_plain: State the definition: Fraction pairs.
Rationals.answer_latex:
Let $\mathbb{Z}$ denote the set of integers and define
\[
W = \mathbb{Z} \times (\mathbb{Z}\setminus\{0\}).
\]
Thus
\[
W = \{(a,b) : a,b\in\mathbb{Z},\ b\neq 0\}.
\]

Each ordered pair $(a,b)$ is intended to represent the formal fraction $a/b$.
Rationals.answer_plain: Let Z denote the set of integers and define \[ W = Z (Z \{0\}). \] Thus \[ W = \{(a,b) : a,b Z,\ b≠ 0\}. \] Each ordered pair (a,b) is intended to represent the formal fraction a/b.

Rationals.label: thm:farey-gap
Rationals.question_latex: State the theorem: Gap Between Farey Neighbors.
Rationals.question_plain: State the theorem: Gap Between Farey Neighbors.
Rationals.answer_latex:
Let

\[
\frac{a}{b} < \frac{c}{d}
\]

be consecutive fractions in a Farey sequence.

Then

\[
\frac{c}{d} - \frac{a}{b}
=
\frac{1}{bd}.
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: Let \[ a{b} < c{d} \] be consecutive fractions in a Farey sequence. Then \[ c{d} - a{b} = 1{bd}. \]

Rationals.label: cor:sb-limit-irrational
Rationals.name_latex: Infinite Stern--Brocot paths converge to irrationals
Rationals.name_plain: Infinite Stern--Brocot paths converge to irrationals
Rationals.question_latex: State the corollary: Infinite Stern--Brocot paths converge to irrationals.
Rationals.question_plain: State the corollary: Infinite Stern--Brocot paths converge to irrationals.
Rationals.answer_latex:
A positive real number $x$ corresponds to a finite path in the
Stern--Brocot tree if and only if $x$ is rational.  Equivalently,
$x$ corresponds to an infinite path if and only if $x$ is irrational.
Rationals.answer_plain: A positive real number x corresponds to a finite path in the Stern--Brocot tree if and only if x is rational. Equivalently, x corresponds to an infinite path if and only if x is irrational.

Rationals.label: cor:sb-limit-irrational
Rationals.name_latex: Infinite Stern--Brocot paths converge to irrationals
Rationals.name_plain: Infinite Stern--Brocot paths converge to irrationals
Rationals.question_latex:
$x \in \mathbb{Q}_{>0}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ $ the Stern–Brocot refinement
process for $x$ terminates in finitely many steps.
Equivalently:
$x \notin \mathbb{Q}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ $ the refinement process
runs for infinitely many steps.
Rationals.question_plain: x ℚ_{>0}\ \ the Stern–Brocot refinement process for x terminates in finitely many steps. Equivalently: x ℚ\ \ the refinement process runs for infinitely many steps.
Rationals.answer_latex: This is the Corollary: Infinite Stern--Brocot paths converge to irrationals
Rationals.answer_plain: This is the Corollary : Infinite Stern--Brocot paths converge to irrationals

Rationals.label: def:mediant
Rationals.question_plain: State the definition: Mediant.
Rationals.answer_latex:
Let
\[
\frac{a}{b},\frac{c}{d}\in\mathbb{Q},
\qquad b,d>0.
\]
The \emph{mediant} of these two fractions is
\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}.
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: Let \[ a{b},c{d} ℚ, b,d>0. \] The mediant of these two fractions is \[ a+c{b+d}. \]

Rationals.label: lem:mediant-gap
Rationals.question_latex: State the lemma: Mediant Gap Lemma.
Rationals.question_plain: State the lemma: Mediant Gap Lemma.
Rationals.answer_latex:
If

\[
\frac{a}{b} < \frac{c}{d},
\]

then the mediant

\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}
\]

lies between them, and the gaps satisfy

\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}-\frac{a}{b}
=
\frac{1}{b(b+d)}
\]

and

\[
\frac{c}{d}-\frac{a+c}{b+d}
=
\frac{1}{d(b+d)}.
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: If \[ a{b} < c{d}, \] then the mediant \[ a+c{b+d} \] lies between them, and the gaps satisfy \[ a+c{b+d}-a{b} = 1{b(b+d)} \] and \[ c{d}-a+c{b+d} = 1{d(b+d)}. \]

Rationals.label: thm:mediant-inequality
Rationals.question_plain: State the theorem: Mediant inequality.
Rationals.answer_latex:
If
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\qquad\text{with}\qquad b,d>0,
\]
then
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{a+c}{b+d}<\frac{c}{d}.
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: If \[ a{b} 0, \] then \[ a{b}<a+c{b+d}<c{d}. \]

Rationals.label: prop:sb-unique-point
Rationals.name_latex: Nested Stern--Brocot intervals contain a unique limit point
Rationals.name_plain: Nested Stern--Brocot intervals contain a unique limit point
Rationals.question_latex: State the proposition: Nested Stern--Brocot intervals contain a unique limit point.
Rationals.question_plain: State the proposition: Nested Stern--Brocot intervals contain a unique limit point.
Rationals.answer_latex:
Let $(I_n)_{n \ge 0}$ be the nested sequence of closed rational intervals
generated by the Stern--Brocot refinement process targeting a real number
$x > 0$, with $I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]$.  Then there exists a unique
real number $\xi \in \bigcap_{n=0}^{\infty} I_n$.
Rationals.answer_plain: Let (I_n)_{n 0} be the nested sequence of closed rational intervals generated by the Stern--Brocot refinement process targeting a real number x > 0, with I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]. Then there exists a unique real number _{n=0}^{∞} I_n.

Rationals.label: def:positive-cone
Rationals.question_plain: State the definition: Positive cone.
Rationals.answer_latex:
Let $F$ be a field.  A subset $P\subset F$ is called a \emph{positive cone} if it satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item if $a,b\in P$, then $a+b\in P$;
  \item if $a,b\in P$, then $ab\in P$;
  \item for every $a\in F$, exactly one of the following holds:
  \[
  a\in P,
  \qquad
  a=0,
  \qquad
  -a\in P.
  \]
\end{enumerate}
Define the order relation by
\[
a<b \iff b-a\in P.
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: Let F be a field. A subset P⊂ F is called a positive cone if it satisfies: enumerate if a,b P, then a+b P; if a,b P, then ab P; for every a F, exactly one of the following holds: \[ a P, a=0, -a P. \] enumerate Define the order relation by \[ a<b iff b-a P. \]

Rationals.label: thm:rational-approximation
Rationals.question_plain: State the theorem: Rational approximation.
Rationals.answer_latex:
For every real number $x$ and every $\varepsilon>0$, there exists $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
|x-r|<\varepsilon.
\]
Rationals.answer_plain: For every real number x and every ε>0, there exists r ℚ such that \[ |x-r|<ε. \]

Rationals.label: def:rational-numbers
Rationals.question_plain: State the definition: Rational numbers.
Rationals.answer_latex:
The set of \emph{rational numbers} is the quotient
\[
\mathbb{Q} = W/\sim.
\]
If $(a,b)\in W$, its equivalence class is denoted by
\[
[(a,b)].
\]
We interpret $[(a,b)]$ as the rational number represented informally by $a/b$.
Rationals.answer_plain: The set of rational numbers is the quotient \[ ℚ = W/ . \] If (a,b) W, its equivalence class is denoted by \[ [(a,b)]. \] We interpret [(a,b)] as the rational number represented informally by a/b.

Rationals.label: def:rational-sequence
Rationals.question_latex: State the definition: Sequence of rational numbers.
Rationals.question_plain: State the definition: Sequence of rational numbers.
Rationals.answer_latex:
A \emph{sequence of rational numbers} is a function
\[
x:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{Q}.
\]
It is usually written $(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ or simply $x_1,x_2,x_3,\dots$.
Rationals.answer_plain: A sequence of rational numbers is a function \[ x:ℕ→ℚ. \] It is usually written (x_n)_{n ℕ} or simply x_1,x_2,x_3, .

Rationals.name_plain: Stern--Brocot interval lengths shrink to zero

Rationals.question_plain: State the lemma: Stern--Brocot interval lengths shrink to zero.
Rationals.answer_latex:
Let $(I_n)_{n \ge 0}$ be the sequence of nested rational intervals generated
by the Stern--Brocot refinement process targeting a real number $x > 0$,
where $I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]$.  Write $\ell_n = c_n/d_n - a_n/b_n$
for the length of $I_n$.  Then $\ell_n \to 0$.
Rationals.answer_plain: Let (I_n)_{n 0} be the sequence of nested rational intervals generated by the Stern--Brocot refinement process targeting a real number x > 0, where I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]. Write _n = c_n/d_n - a_n/b_n for the length of I_n. Then _n → 0.

Rationals.label: thm:stern-brocot-refinement
Rationals.question_latex: State the theorem: Stern--Brocot interval refinement.
Rationals.question_plain: State the theorem: Stern--Brocot interval refinement.
Rationals.answer_latex:
Let $x>0$ be a real number.  Suppose one begins with two rational
numbers

\[
\frac{a}{b} < x < \frac{c}{d},
\qquad b,d>0,
\]

and repeatedly inserts the mediant

\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}.
\]

At each step, replace one endpoint by the mediant so that the new
interval still contains $x$.  Then this process generates a nested
sequence of rational intervals containing $x$.
Rationals.answer_plain: Let x>0 be a real number. Suppose one begins with two rational numbers \[ a{b} 0, \] and repeatedly inserts the mediant \[ a+c{b+d}. \] At each step, replace one endpoint by the mediant so that the new interval still contains x. Then this process generates a nested sequence of rational intervals containing x.

Real Valued Functions.label: def:pointwise-ops-R
Real Valued Functions.question_latex: Define the pointwise sum of two real-valued functions $f,g : X \to \mathbb{R}$.
Real Valued Functions.question_plain: Define the pointwise sum of two real-valued functions f,g : X → ℝ.
Real Valued Functions.answer_latex: $(f+g)(x) := f(x) + g(x)$ for all $x \in X$. The operations $-, \cdot, /, \max, \min, c\cdot$ are defined analogously.
Real Valued Functions.answer_plain: (f+g)(x) := f(x) + g(x) for all x X. The operations -, , /, , , c are defined analogously.
Real Valued Functions.hint: Pointwise = apply the operation value-by-value. No limits involved.

Real Valued Functions.label: def:pointwise-ops-R
Real Valued Functions.question_latex: Each operation is pointwise: the value of the combined function at $x$ depends only on $f(x)$ and $g(x)$, not on nearby values. Formally, $f + g$ is the unique function $h : X \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $h(x) = f(x) + g(x)$ for all $x \in X$.
Real Valued Functions.question_plain: Each operation is pointwise : the value of the combined function at x depends only on f(x) and g(x), not on nearby values. Formally, f + g is the unique function h : X → ℝ such that h(x) = f(x) + g(x) for all x X.
Real Valued Functions.answer_latex: This is the Definition: Arithmetic Operations on Real-Valued Functions
Real Valued Functions.answer_plain: This is the Definition : Arithmetic Operations on Real-Valued Functions

Real Valued Functions.label: def:pointwise-ops-R

Real Valued Functions.question_plain: Give a failure case or negation for: Arithmetic Operations on Real-Valued Functions.
Real Valued Functions.answer_latex: $fg$ is not composition: $(fg)(x) = f(x)g(x)$ but $(f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x))$. These agree only in special cases.

Reals · Constructions.label: thm:stern-brocot-limit
Reals · Constructions.question_latex: State the theorem: Stern–Brocot Limit Theorem.
Reals · Constructions.question_plain: State the theorem: Stern–Brocot Limit Theorem.
Reals · Constructions.answer_latex:
Let

\[
\left(\frac{a_n}{b_n}\right),\quad
\left(\frac{c_n}{d_n}\right)
\]

be sequences of rationals obtained by successive Stern–Brocot
refinement so that

\[
\frac{a_n}{b_n}
<
\frac{c_n}{d_n}
\]

and

\[
\left[\frac{a_{n+1}}{b_{n+1}},\frac{c_{n+1}}{d_{n+1}}\right]
\subseteq
\left[\frac{a_n}{b_n},\frac{c_n}{d_n}\right].
\]

Then there exists a unique real number

\[
x
=
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{a_n}{b_n}
=
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{c_n}{d_n}.
\]
Reals · Constructions.answer_plain: Let \[ (a_n{b_n} ), (c_n{d_n} ) \] be sequences of rationals obtained by successive Stern–Brocot refinement so that \[ a_n{b_n} < c_n{d_n} \] and \[ ⊆ . \] Then there exists a unique real number \[ x = _{n→∞}a_n{b_n} = _{n→∞}c_n{d_n}. \]

Reals · Foundations.question_latex: State the theorem: Cantor's Intersection Theorem.
Reals · Foundations.question_plain: State the theorem: Cantor's Intersection Theorem.
Reals · Foundations.answer_latex:
Let $(X, d)$ be a metric space and let
\[
  A_1 \supseteq A_2 \supseteq A_3 \supseteq \cdots
\]
be a nested sequence of nonempty compact subsets of $X$. Then
\[
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n
\]
is compact and nonempty.

\[

  \quad\text{and}\quad
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \text{ is compact.}
\]

Sequences.label: thm:algebra-cauchy
Sequences.question_latex: Sketch the proof of: T6.4 — Algebra of Cauchy sequences.
Sequences.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: T6.4 — Algebra of Cauchy sequences.
Sequences.answer_latex: Cases 1, 2, 5 are direct triangle-inequality arguments. Case 3 uses the add-and-subtract decomposition $a_m b_m - a_n b_n = a_m(b_m-b_n) + b_n(a_m-a_n)$ together with the boundedness of Cauchy sequences (T6.2). Case 4 uses the uniform lower bound $|b_n| \ge \delta$ to control the denominator. No limits are named anywhere: this is an intrinsic, internal argument.
Sequences.answer_plain: Cases 1, 2, 5 are direct triangle-inequality arguments. Case 3 uses the add-and-subtract decomposition a_m b_m - a_n b_n = a_m(b_m-b_n) + b_n(a_m-a_n) together with the boundedness of Cauchy sequences (T6.2). Case 4 uses the uniform lower bound |b_n| to control the denominator. No limits are named anywhere: this is an intrinsic, internal argument.

Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.answer_plain: The set ℚ of rational numbers is countable.

Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.answer_plain: The set ℝ of real numbers is not countable.

Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.label: thm:cantor
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.answer_latex:
For any set $A$, there is no surjection $A \to \mathcal{P}(A)$. In
particular, $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$.
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.answer_plain: For any set A, there is no surjection A → P(A). In particular, |A| < |P(A)|.

Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.label: thm:cantor
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.question_latex: $\forall A:\; \neg\exists f: A\to\mathcal{P}(A),\; \forall S\in\mathcal{P}(A),\; \exists a\in A,\; f(a)=S$. Equivalently: $\forall f: A\to\mathcal{P}(A),\; \exists D\in\mathcal{P}(A),\; \forall a\in A,\; f(a)\neq D$.
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.question_plain: A:\; f: A→P(A),\; S P(A),\; a A,\; f(a)=S. Equivalently: f: A→P(A),\; D P(A),\; a A,\; f(a)≠ D.
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : Cantor's Theorem

Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.label: thm:countable-union
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.name_latex: Countable union of countable sets is countable
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.name_plain: Countable union of countable sets is countable
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.question_latex: State the theorem: Countable union of countable sets is countable.
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.question_plain: State the theorem: Countable union of countable sets is countable.
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.answer_latex:
Let $\{A_n\}_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ be a countable family of countable sets.
Then $\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n$ is countable.
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.answer_plain: Let \{A_n\}_{n ℕ} be a countable family of countable sets. Then _{n=1}^{∞} A_n is countable.

Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.label: thm:schroder-bernstein
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.answer_latex:
If $|A| \leq |B|$ and $|B| \leq |A|$, then $A \sim B$.

Equivalently: if there exist injections $f : A \hookrightarrow B$ and
$g : B \hookrightarrow A$, then there exists a bijection $h : A \to B$.
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.answer_plain: If |A| ≤ |B| and |B| ≤ |A|, then A B. Equivalently: if there exist injections f : A B and g : B A, then there exists a bijection h : A → B.

Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.label: thm:schroder-bernstein
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.question_latex: $\bigl(\exists f: A\hookrightarrow B\bigr) \wedge \bigl(\exists g: B\hookrightarrow A\bigr) \;\to\; \exists h: A\to B,\; h$ bijective.
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.question_plain: ( f: A B ) ( g: B A ) \;→\; h: A→ B,\; h bijective.
Sets Relations Functions · Cardinality.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : Schröder--Bernstein Theorem

Sets Relations Functions · Equivalence.label: thm:equiv-partition
Sets Relations Functions · Equivalence.question_latex: State the theorem: Equivalence Relations and Partitions.
Sets Relations Functions · Equivalence.question_plain: State the theorem: Equivalence Relations and Partitions.
Sets Relations Functions · Equivalence.answer_latex:
Let $A$ be a set.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item If $R$ is an equivalence relation on $A$, then $A/R$ is a partition of $A$.
\item If $\mathcal{P}$ is a partition of $A$, then the relation
$R_{\mathcal{P}}$ defined by
\[
(a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\exists P \in \mathcal{P} \text{ with } a \in P \text{ and } b \in P
\]
is an equivalence relation on $A$.
\item These constructions are inverse: $R_{A/R} = R$ and $A/R_{\mathcal{P}} = \mathcal{P}$.
\end{enumerate}
Sets Relations Functions · Equivalence.answer_plain: Let A be a set. enumerate[label=( )] If R is an equivalence relation on A, then A/R is a partition of A. If P is a partition of A, then the relation R_{P} defined by \[ (a,b) R_{P} \; \; P P with a P and b P \] is an equivalence relation on A. These constructions are inverse: R_{A/R} = R and A/R_{P} = P. enumerate

Sets Relations Functions · Equivalence.label: thm:quotient-universal
Sets Relations Functions · Equivalence.name_latex: Universal Property of the Quotient Map
Sets Relations Functions · Equivalence.name_plain: Universal Property of the Quotient Map
Sets Relations Functions · Equivalence.question_latex: State the theorem: Universal Property of the Quotient Map.
Sets Relations Functions · Equivalence.question_plain: State the theorem: Universal Property of the Quotient Map.
Sets Relations Functions · Equivalence.answer_latex:
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$, let $\pi : A \to A/R$ be the
canonical surjection, and let $f : A \to B$ be any function. Then $f$ is
\emph{constant on equivalence classes} — meaning $(a,b) \in R$ implies
$f(a) = f(b)$ — if and only if there exists a unique function
$\bar{f} : A/R \to B$ such that
\[
f = \bar{f} \circ \pi.
\]
When it exists, $\bar{f}$ is defined by $\bar{f}([a]) = f(a)$.
Sets Relations Functions · Equivalence.answer_plain: Let R be an equivalence relation on A, let : A → A/R be the canonical surjection, and let f : A → B be any function. Then f is constant on equivalence classes — meaning (a,b) R implies f(a) = f(b) — if and only if there exists a unique function f : A/R → B such that \[ f = f . \] When it exists, f is defined by f([a]) = f(a).

Sets Relations Functions · Equivalence.answer_latex: This is the Theorem: Universal Property of the Quotient Map
Sets Relations Functions · Equivalence.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : Universal Property of the Quotient Map

Sets Relations Functions · Families.label: prop:fip-duality
Sets Relations Functions · Families.answer_latex:
Let $X$ be a set, $A \subseteq X$, and $\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}$ a
family of subsets of $X$. Let $F_\alpha = U_\alpha^c = X \setminus U_\alpha$.
Then:
\[
\{U_\alpha\} \text{ covers } A
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
\{F_\alpha \cap A\}_{\alpha \in I} \text{ does \emph{not} have the FIP}.
\]
More precisely: the family $\{U_\alpha\}$ does \emph{not} cover $A$ if and
only if the family of closed complements $\{F_\alpha\}$ has the FIP relative
to $A$.
Sets Relations Functions · Families.answer_plain: Let X be a set, A ⊆ X, and \{U_ \}_{ I} a family of subsets of X. Let F_ = U_ ^c = X U_ . Then: \[ \{U_ \} covers A \;\; \;\; \{F_ A\}_{ I} does \emph{not have the FIP}. \] More precisely: the family \{U_ \} does not cover A if and only if the family of closed complements \{F_ \} has the FIP relative to A.

Reading pointwise: $\forall x\in A,\;\exists\alpha\in I,\; x\in U_\alpha \;\leftrightarrow\; \{F_\alpha\cap A\}$ fails the FIP.

Sets Relations Functions · Functions.question_latex: State the theorem: Images and Set Operations.
Sets Relations Functions · Functions.question_plain: State the theorem: Images and Set Operations.
Sets Relations Functions · Functions.answer_latex:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq A$. Then
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f(S \cup T) = f(S) \cup f(T)$,
\item $f(S \cap T) \subseteq f(S) \cap f(T)$,
\item $f(S \setminus T) \supseteq f(S) \setminus f(T)$.
\end{enumerate}
Equality holds in (ii) and (iii) for all $S,T$ iff $f$ is injective.

(ii) $\forall b\in B:\; (\exists a\in S\cap T,\; f(a)=b) \to (\exists a\in S,\; f(a)=b) \wedge (\exists a\in T,\; f(a)=b)$; equality iff $f$ injective.

Sets Relations Functions · Functions.label: thm:comp-inj-surj

Sets Relations Functions · Functions.question_plain: State the theorem: Injectivity and Surjectivity Under Composition.
Sets Relations Functions · Functions.answer_latex:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item If $f$ and $g$ are injective, then $g \circ f$ is injective.
\item If $f$ and $g$ are surjective, then $g \circ f$ is surjective.
\item If $g \circ f$ is injective, then $f$ is injective.
\item If $g \circ f$ is surjective, then $g$ is surjective.
\end{enumerate}
Sets Relations Functions · Functions.answer_plain: Let f : A → B and g : B → C. enumerate[label=( )] If f and g are injective, then g f is injective. If f and g are surjective, then g f is surjective. If g f is injective, then f is injective. If g f is surjective, then g is surjective. enumerate

(iii) $(\forall a_1,a_2\in A,\; g(f(a_1))=g(f(a_2))\to a_1=a_2) \;\to\; \forall a_1,a_2\in A,\; f(a_1)=f(a_2)\to a_1=a_2$.

Sets Relations Functions · Functions.answer_latex:
Let $f : A \to B$.

\item $f$ has a right inverse $\iff$ $f$ is surjective.
\item If $f$ has both a left inverse $g$ and a right inverse $h$, then $g = h$
and $f$ is bijective.
\end{enumerate}

  \item for every $v \in A$ with $v < u^*$, there exists $s \in S$ with
        $v < s$.

Sets Relations Functions · Order.label: def:comparable
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_latex:
$a,b$ comparable $\;\leftrightarrow\; a \leq b \vee b \leq a$. \quad
$a,b$ incomparable $\;\leftrightarrow\; \neg(a \leq b) \wedge \neg(b \leq a)$.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_plain: a,b comparable \; \; a ≤ b b ≤ a. a,b incomparable \; \; (a ≤ b) (b ≤ a).
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_latex: This is the Definition: Comparable and Incomparable Elements
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_plain: This is the Definition : Comparable and Incomparable Elements

Sets Relations Functions · Order.label: prop:order-duality
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_latex: State the proposition: Duality Principle for Posets.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_plain: State the proposition: Duality Principle for Posets.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_latex:
Let $\Phi$ be any first-order statement about a poset $(A, \leq)$ expressed
using only the relation $\leq$.  Let $\Phi^*$ be the statement obtained from
$\Phi$ by replacing every occurrence of $\leq$ with $\geq$ (equivalently,
working in the dual poset).  Then:
\[
  (A, \leq) \models \Phi
  \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
  (A, \geq) \models \Phi.
\]
In particular, if $\Phi$ is a theorem about all posets, then so is $\Phi^*$.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_plain: Let be any first-order statement about a poset (A, ≤) expressed using only the relation ≤. Let ^* be the statement obtained from by replacing every occurrence of ≤ with ≥ (equivalently, working in the dual poset). Then: \[ (A, ≤) \;\; \;\; (A, ≥) . \] In particular, if is a theorem about all posets, then so is ^*.

Sets Relations Functions · Order.label: prop:order-duality
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_latex:
$\forall$ posets $(A,\leq)$, $\forall$ first-order sentences $\Phi$ in the
language of $\leq$: $(A,\leq)\models\Phi \;\leftrightarrow\; (A,\geq)\models\Phi^*$,
where $\Phi^*$ substitutes $\geq$ for every $\leq$.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_plain: posets (A,≤), first-order sentences in the language of ≤: (A,≤) \; \; (A,≥) ^*, where ^* substitutes ≥ for every ≤.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_latex: This is the Proposition: Duality Principle for Posets
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_plain: This is the Proposition : Duality Principle for Posets

Sets Relations Functions · Order.label: prop:sup-inf-duality
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_latex: State the proposition: Duality of Supremum and Infimum.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_plain: State the proposition: Duality of Supremum and Infimum.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_latex:
Let $(A, \leq)$ be a poset and $S \subseteq A$.  Then
$\inf_{(A,\leq)} S = \sup_{(A,\geq)} S$,
where $(A,\geq)$ denotes the dual poset.

In particular: if every nonempty bounded-above subset of $(A,\leq)$ has a
supremum, then every nonempty bounded-below subset of $(A,\leq)$ has an
infimum.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_plain: Let (A, ≤) be a poset and S ⊆ A. Then _{(A,≤)} S = _{(A,≥)} S, where (A,≥) denotes the dual poset. In particular: if every nonempty bounded-above subset of (A,≤) has a supremum, then every nonempty bounded-below subset of (A,≤) has an infimum.

Sets Relations Functions · Order.label: prop:sup-inf-duality
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_latex:
$\forall S \subseteq A$: $\ell^*$ is the infimum of $S$ in $(A,\leq)$
$\;\leftrightarrow\;$ $\ell^*$ is the supremum of $S$ in $(A,\geq)$.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_plain: S ⊆ A: ^* is the infimum of S in (A,≤) \; \; ^* is the supremum of S in (A,≥).
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_latex: This is the Proposition: Duality of Supremum and Infimum
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_plain: This is the Proposition : Duality of Supremum and Infimum

Sets Relations Functions · Order.label: def:initial-segment
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_latex: State the definition: Initial Segment.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_plain: State the definition: Initial Segment.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_latex:
A subset $I \subseteq A$ is an \emph{initial segment} of $(A,\leq)$ if
\[
a \in I \text{ and } b \leq a \;\Rightarrow\; b \in I.
\]
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_plain: A subset I ⊆ A is an initial segment of (A,≤) if \[ a I and b ≤ a \;⇒\; b I. \]

Sets Relations Functions · Order.label: def:initial-segment
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_latex: $I$ is an initial segment $\;\leftrightarrow\; \forall a \in I,\; \forall b \in A,\; b \leq a \to b \in I$.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_plain: I is an initial segment \; \; a I,\; b A,\; b ≤ a → b I.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_latex: This is the Definition: Initial Segment
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_plain: This is the Definition : Initial Segment

Sets Relations Functions · Order.label: prop:embedding-iso
Sets Relations Functions · Order.name_latex: Order embedding is isomorphism onto image
Sets Relations Functions · Order.name_plain: Order embedding is isomorphism onto image
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_latex: State the proposition: Order embedding is isomorphism onto image.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_plain: State the proposition: Order embedding is isomorphism onto image.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_latex:
If $f : (A, \leq) \to (B, \leq')$ is an order embedding, then $f$ is an
order isomorphism from $(A, \leq)$ to the suborder $(f(A), \leq'_{f(A)})$.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_plain: If f : (A, ≤) → (B, ≤') is an order embedding, then f is an order isomorphism from (A, ≤) to the suborder (f(A), ≤'_{f(A)}).

Sets Relations Functions · Order.label: prop:embedding-iso
Sets Relations Functions · Order.name_latex: Order embedding is isomorphism onto image
Sets Relations Functions · Order.name_plain: Order embedding is isomorphism onto image
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_latex: $\forall x,y \in A:\; x \leq y \;\leftrightarrow\; f(x) \leq'_{f(A)} f(y)$, i.e.\ $f$ is an order isomorphism $(A,\leq)\cong(f(A),\leq')$.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_plain: x,y A:\; x ≤ y \; \; f(x) ≤'_{f(A)} f(y), i.e.\ f is an order isomorphism (A,≤) (f(A),≤').
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_latex: This is the Proposition: Order embedding is isomorphism onto image
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_plain: This is the Proposition : Order embedding is isomorphism onto image

Sets Relations Functions · Order.label: prop:embedding-injective
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_latex: State the proposition: Order embeddings are injective.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_plain: State the proposition: Order embeddings are injective.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_latex: Every order embedding $f : (A, \leq) \to (B, \leq')$ is injective.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_plain: Every order embedding f : (A, ≤) → (B, ≤') is injective.

Sets Relations Functions · Order.label: prop:embedding-injective
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_latex: $\forall x,y \in A:\; f(x) = f(y) \to x = y$.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.question_plain: x,y A:\; f(x) = f(y) → x = y.
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_latex: This is the Proposition: Order embeddings are injective
Sets Relations Functions · Order.answer_plain: This is the Proposition : Order embeddings are injective

Sets Relations Functions · Relations.label: def:antisymmetric
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_latex:
$R$ is \emph{antisymmetric} if mutual relation implies equality:
\[
\forall a,b \in A, \;
\bigl( (a,b) \in R \land (b,a) \in R \bigr) \rightarrow a = b.
\]
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_plain: R is antisymmetric if mutual relation implies equality: \[ a,b A, \; ( (a,b) R ∧ (b,a) R ) a = b. \]

Sets Relations Functions · Relations.label: def:asymmetric
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_latex:
$R$ is \emph{asymmetric} if related pairs never commute:
\[
\forall a,b \in A, \;
(a,b) \in R \rightarrow (b,a) \notin R.
\]
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_plain: R is asymmetric if related pairs never commute: \[ a,b A, \; (a,b) R (b,a) R. \]

Sets Relations Functions · Relations.label: def:irreflexive
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_latex:
$R$ is \emph{irreflexive} if no element is related to itself:
\[
\forall a \in A, \; (a,a) \notin R.
\]
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_plain: R is irreflexive if no element is related to itself: \[ a A, \; (a,a) R. \]

Sets Relations Functions · Relations.label: def:reflexive
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_latex:
A relation $R$ on $A$ is \emph{reflexive} if every element is related to itself:
\[
\forall a \in A, \; (a,a) \in R.
\]
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_plain: A relation R on A is reflexive if every element is related to itself: \[ a A, \; (a,a) R. \]

Sets Relations Functions · Relations.label: def:symmetric
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_latex:
$R$ is \emph{symmetric} if related pairs commute:
\[
\forall a,b \in A, \; (a,b) \in R \rightarrow (b,a) \in R.
\]
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_plain: R is symmetric if related pairs commute: \[ a,b A, \; (a,b) R (b,a) R. \]

Sets Relations Functions · Relations.label: def:total-rel
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_latex:
$R$ is \emph{total} (or \emph{connex}) if every pair is comparable:
\[
\forall a,b \in A, \;
(a,b) \in R \lor (b,a) \in R.
\]
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_plain: R is total (or connex) if every pair is comparable: \[ a,b A, \; (a,b) R ∨ (b,a) R. \]

Sets Relations Functions · Relations.label: def:total-rel
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.question_latex:
$R$ is a preorder on $A$ iff:
\[
(\forall a \in A,\; (a,a)\in R)\;\wedge\;
(\forall a,b,c \in A,\; (a,b)\in R \wedge (b,c)\in R \to (a,c)\in R).
\]
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.question_plain: R is a preorder on A iff: \[ ( a A,\; (a,a) R)\; \; ( a,b,c A,\; (a,b) R (b,c) R → (a,c) R). \]
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_plain: This is the Definition : Total (Connex) Relation

Sets Relations Functions · Relations.label: def:transitive
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_latex:
$R$ is \emph{transitive} if it extends along chains:
\[
\forall a,b,c \in A, \;
\bigl( (a,b) \in R \land (b,c) \in R \bigr) \rightarrow (a,c) \in R.
\]
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_plain: R is transitive if it extends along chains: \[ a,b,c A, \; ( (a,b) R ∧ (b,c) R ) (a,c) R. \]

Sets Relations Functions · Relations.label: thm:ordered-pair-unique
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.question_latex: State the theorem: Uniqueness of Ordered Pairs.
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.question_plain: State the theorem: Uniqueness of Ordered Pairs.
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_latex:
For any sets $a,b,c,d$,
\[
(a,b) = (c,d)
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
(a = c \land b = d).
\]
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_plain: For any sets a,b,c,d, \[ (a,b) = (c,d) \;\; \;\; (a = c ∧ b = d). \]

Sets Relations Functions · Relations.label: thm:ordered-pair-unique
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.question_latex: $\forall a, b, c, d:\; (a,b) = (c,d) \;\leftrightarrow\; (a = c \wedge b = d)$.
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_latex: This is the Theorem: Uniqueness of Ordered Pairs
Sets Relations Functions · Relations.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : Uniqueness of Ordered Pairs

Sets Relations Functions · Set Systems.label: thm:sigma-intersection
Sets Relations Functions · Set Systems.question_latex: State the theorem: Intersection of $\sigma$-algebras is a $\sigma$-algebra.
Sets Relations Functions · Set Systems.question_plain: State the theorem: Intersection of -algebras is a -algebra.
Sets Relations Functions · Set Systems.answer_latex:
Let $\{\mathcal{M}_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}$ be any family of $\sigma$-algebras
on $X$ (indexed by any set $I$). Then
\[
\mathcal{M} \;:=\; \bigcap_{\alpha \in I} \mathcal{M}_\alpha
\]
is a $\sigma$-algebra on $X$.
Sets Relations Functions · Set Systems.answer_plain: Let \{M_ \}_{ I} be any family of -algebras on X (indexed by any set I). Then \[ M \;:=\; _{ I} M_ \] is a -algebra on X.

Sets Relations Functions · Set Systems.label: thm:sigma-intersection
Sets Relations Functions · Set Systems.question_latex:
$\bigl(\forall\alpha\in I,\; \mathcal{M}_\alpha \text{ is a $\sigma$-algebra on }X\bigr) \;\to\; \bigcap_{\alpha\in I}\mathcal{M}_\alpha \text{ is a $\sigma$-algebra on }X$.\\
Explicitly: $X\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha$; $A\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha \to A^c\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha$; $\forall n,\; A_n\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha \to \bigcup_n A_n\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha$.
Sets Relations Functions · Set Systems.question_plain: ( I,\; M_ is a \sigma-algebra on X ) \;→\; _{ I}M_ is a \sigma-algebra on X.\\ Explicitly: X M_ ; A M_ → A^c M_ ; n,\; A_n M_ → _n A_n M_ .
Sets Relations Functions · Set Systems.answer_latex: This is the Theorem: Intersection of -algebras is a -algebra
Sets Relations Functions · Set Systems.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : Intersection of -algebras is a -algebra

Sets Relations Functions · Sets.label: thm:associativity
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_latex: State the theorem: Associativity of Union and Intersection.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_plain: State the theorem: Associativity of Union and Intersection.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_latex:
Let $A,B,C$ be sets. Then
\[
(A \cup B) \cup C = A \cup (B \cup C)
\quad\text{and}\quad
(A \cap B) \cap C = A \cap (B \cap C).
\]
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_plain: Let A,B,C be sets. Then \[ (A B) C = A (B C) and (A B) C = A (B C). \]

Sets Relations Functions · Sets.label: thm:associativity
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_latex: $\forall A, B, C:\; \forall x\,\bigl((x \in (A \cup B) \cup C) \leftrightarrow (x \in A \cup (B \cup C))\bigr)$, and analogously for $\cap$.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_plain: A, B, C:\; x\, ((x (A B) C) (x A (B C)) ), and analogously for .
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_latex: This is the Theorem: Associativity of Union and Intersection
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : Associativity of Union and Intersection

Sets Relations Functions · Sets.label: thm:commutativity
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_latex: State the theorem: Commutativity of Union and Intersection.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_plain: State the theorem: Commutativity of Union and Intersection.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_latex:
Let $A,B$ be sets. Then
\[
A \cup B = B \cup A
\quad\text{and}\quad
A \cap B = B \cap A.
\]
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_plain: Let A,B be sets. Then \[ A B = B A and A B = B A. \]

Sets Relations Functions · Sets.label: thm:commutativity
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_latex: $\forall A, B:\; \forall x\,(x \in A \cup B \leftrightarrow x \in B \cup A)$ and $\forall x\,(x \in A \cap B \leftrightarrow x \in B \cap A)$.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_plain: A, B:\; x\,(x A B x B A) and x\,(x A B x B A).
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_latex: This is the Theorem: Commutativity of Union and Intersection
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : Commutativity of Union and Intersection

Sets Relations Functions · Sets.label: thm:distributivity
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_latex:
Let $A,B,C$ be sets. Then
\[
A \cap (B \cup C) = (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap C),
\]
\[
A \cup (B \cap C) = (A \cup B) \cap (A \cup C).
\]
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_plain: Let A,B,C be sets. Then \[ A (B C) = (A B) (A C), \] \[ A (B C) = (A B) (A C). \]

Sets Relations Functions · Sets.label: thm:distributivity
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_latex: $\forall A, B, C:\; \forall x\,\bigl(x \in A \cap (B \cup C) \leftrightarrow x \in (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap C)\bigr)$, and dually for $\cup$ over $\cap$.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_plain: A, B, C:\; x\, (x A (B C) x (A B) (A C) ), and dually for over .
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : Distributive Laws

Sets Relations Functions · Sets.label: thm:identity-absorption
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_latex: State the theorem: Identity and Absorption Laws.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_plain: State the theorem: Identity and Absorption Laws.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_latex:
Let $A,B$ be sets and $U$ a universe with $A \subseteq U$. Then
\[
A \cup \varnothing = A,
\qquad
A \cap U = A,
\]
\[
A \cup (A \cap B) = A,
\qquad
A \cap (A \cup B) = A.
\]
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_plain: Let A,B be sets and U a universe with A ⊆ U. Then \[ A = A, A U = A, \] \[ A (A B) = A, A (A B) = A. \]

Sets Relations Functions · Sets.label: thm:identity-absorption
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_latex:
$\forall A \subseteq U:\; \forall x\,(x \in A \cup \varnothing \leftrightarrow x \in A)$ and $\forall x\,(x \in A \cap U \leftrightarrow x \in A)$.
Absorption: $\forall A, B:\; \forall x\,(x \in A \cup (A \cap B) \leftrightarrow x \in A)$, and dually.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_plain: A ⊆ U:\; x\,(x A x A) and x\,(x A U x A). Absorption: A, B:\; x\,(x A (A B) x A), and dually.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_latex: This is the Theorem: Identity and Absorption Laws
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : Identity and Absorption Laws

Sets Relations Functions · Sets.label: cor:set-duality
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_latex: State the corollary: Principle of Set Duality.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_plain: State the corollary: Principle of Set Duality.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_latex:
Any identity involving $\cup$, $\cap$, $\varnothing$, and $U$ that holds for
all subsets of a universe remains valid when each operation and constant is
replaced by its dual.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_plain: Any identity involving , , , and U that holds for all subsets of a universe remains valid when each operation and constant is replaced by its dual.

Sets Relations Functions · Sets.label: cor:set-duality
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_latex: For any identity $\forall A_1,…,A_n \subseteq U:\; E(A_i,\cup,\cap,\varnothing,U) = E'(A_i,\cup,\cap,\varnothing,U)$, the dual identity $\forall A_1,…,A_n \subseteq U:\; E^d = E'^d$ holds, where $E^d$ replaces $\cup\leftrightarrow\cap$ and $\varnothing\leftrightarrow U$.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_plain: For any identity A_1,…,A_n ⊆ U:\; E(A_i, , , ,U) = E'(A_i, , , ,U), the dual identity A_1,…,A_n ⊆ U:\; E^d = E'^d holds, where E^d replaces and U.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_latex: This is the Corollary: Principle of Set Duality
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_plain: This is the Corollary : Principle of Set Duality

Sets Relations Functions · Sets.label: def:set-duality
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_latex:
Two set-theoretic expressions over a fixed universe $U$ are \emph{dual} if one
is obtained from the other by simultaneously replacing
$\cup \leftrightarrow \cap$ and $\varnothing \leftrightarrow U$, with
complements unchanged.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_plain: Two set-theoretic expressions over a fixed universe U are dual if one is obtained from the other by simultaneously replacing and U, with complements unchanged.

Sets Relations Functions · Sets.label: def:set-duality
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_latex: $\forall$ expressions $E, E'$ over $U$: $E$ and $E'$ are dual iff $E'$ is obtained from $E$ by the substitution $\cup \mapsto \cap,\; \cap \mapsto \cup,\; \varnothing \mapsto U,\; U \mapsto \varnothing$.
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.question_plain: expressions E, E' over U: E and E' are dual iff E' is obtained from E by the substitution ,\; ,\; U,\; U .
Sets Relations Functions · Sets.answer_plain: This is the Definition : Set Duality

Subsets Real Line.question_latex: Define a bounded subset of $\mathbb{R}$.
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: Define a bounded subset of ℝ.
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex:
$X$ is bounded if $X \subseteq [-M,M]$ for some $M > 0$,
  i.e.\ $\exists M > 0, \forall x\in X, |x|\leq M$.
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: X is bounded if X ⊆ [-M,M] for some M > 0, i.e.\ M > 0, x X, |x|≤ M.

Subsets Real Line.answer_latex:
$E \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is closed if $\overline{E} = E$:
  every adherent point of $E$ lies in $E$.
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: E ⊆ ℝ is closed if E = E: every adherent point of E lies in E.
Subsets Real Line.hint:
Closed $=$ contains all its limit points $=$ sequentially
  closed.

Subsets Real Line.label: def:closed-set-R
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex: This is the Definition: Closed Subset of ℝ
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: This is the Definition : Closed Subset of ℝ

Subsets Real Line.label: def:closed-set-R
Subsets Real Line.question_latex: Give a failure case or negation for: Closed Subset of $\mathbb{R}$.
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: Give a failure case or negation for: Closed Subset of ℝ.
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex:
$(a,b)$ is not closed: $a$ and $b$ are adherent to
  $(a,b)$ but $a,b \notin (a,b)$.
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: (a,b) is not closed: a and b are adherent to (a,b) but a,b (a,b).

Subsets Real Line.label: def:closed-set-R
Subsets Real Line.question_latex: What does Closed Subset of $\mathbb{R}$ immediately give you?
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: What does Closed Subset of ℝ immediately give you?

Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: cor:closed-iff-seq-limits

Subsets Real Line.label: def:closure-R
Subsets Real Line.name_plain: Closure of a Subset of ℝ
Subsets Real Line.question_latex:
Define the closure $\overline{X}$ of
  $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$.
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: Define the closure X of X ⊆ ℝ.
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex:
$\overline{X} := \{x \in \mathbb{R} :
  \forall \varepsilon > 0\;\exists y \in X: |x-y|\leq\varepsilon\}$
  — the set of all adherent points of $X$.
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: X := \{x ℝ : ε > 0\; y X: |x-y|≤ε\} — the set of all adherent points of X.

Subsets Real Line.label: def:closure-R
Subsets Real Line.name_plain: Closure of a Subset of ℝ
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex: This is the Definition: Closure of a Subset of ℝ
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: This is the Definition : Closure of a Subset of ℝ

Subsets Real Line.label: def:closure-R
Subsets Real Line.name_plain: Closure of a Subset of ℝ
Subsets Real Line.question_latex: Give a failure case or negation for: Closure of a Subset of $\mathbb{R}$.
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: Give a failure case or negation for: Closure of a Subset of ℝ.
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex:
$\overline{\mathbb{Q}} \neq \mathbb{Q}$: every real is
  adherent to $\mathbb{Q}$ by density of $\mathbb{Q}$ in
  $\mathbb{R}$.
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: ℚ ≠ ℚ: every real is adherent to ℚ by density of ℚ in ℝ.
