Subsets Real Line.label: lem:closure-elementary
Subsets Real Line.question_latex:
State the four elementary properties of closures
  (Tao Lemma 9.1.11).
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: State the four elementary properties of closures (Tao Lemma 9.1.11).
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex:
(i) $X \subseteq \overline{X}$;\quad
  (ii) $\overline{X\cup Y}=\overline{X}\cup\overline{Y}$;\quad
  (iii) $\overline{X\cap Y}\subseteq
    \overline{X}\cap\overline{Y}$;\quad
  (iv) $X\subseteq Y\Rightarrow\overline{X}\subseteq\overline{Y}$.
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: (i) X ⊆ X; (ii) X\cup Y=X Y; (iii) X\cap Y⊆ X Y; (iv) X⊆ Y⇒X⊆Y.

Subsets Real Line.label: lem:closure-elementary
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: \[ X, Y ⊆ ℝ: cases x X,\; x X \\ X \cup Y = X Y \\ x X \cap Y,\; x X Y \\ X ⊆ Y \;⇒\; x X,\; x Y cases \]
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex: This is the Lemma: Elementary Properties of Closures
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: This is the Lemma : Elementary Properties of Closures

Subsets Real Line.label: lem:closure-elementary
Subsets Real Line.question_latex:
Give a failure case or negation for: Elementary Properties of
  Closures.
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: Give a failure case or negation for: Elementary Properties of Closures.
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex:
$\overline{X\cap Y} \neq \overline{X}\cap\overline{Y}$
  in general: $X=(0,1)$, $Y=(1,2)$ gives
  $\overline{X\cap Y}=\varnothing$ but
  $\overline{X}\cap\overline{Y}=\{1\}$.
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: X\cap Y ≠ X Y in general: X=(0,1), Y=(1,2) gives X\cap Y= but X Y=\{1\}.

Subsets Real Line.label: lem:closure-elementary
Subsets Real Line.question_latex:
What does Elementary Properties of
  Closures immediately give you?
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: What does Elementary Properties of Closures immediately give you?

Subsets Real Line.label: cor:interval-all-limit-points
Subsets Real Line.name_latex: Every Point of an Interval is a Limit Point
Subsets Real Line.name_plain: Every Point of an Interval is a Limit Point
Subsets Real Line.question_latex: State the corollary: Every Point of an Interval is a Limit Point.
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: State the corollary: Every Point of an Interval is a Limit Point.
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: TaoAnalysis1 Let I be an interval. Then every element of I is a limit point of I.

Subsets Real Line.label: thm:heine-borel
Subsets Real Line.question_latex: Define: property $P$ is true near $x_0$.
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: Define: property P is true near x_0.
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex:
There exists $\delta > 0$ such that $P(f(x), …)$
  holds for all $x \in I$ with $0 < |x - x_0| < \delta$.
  Written $P(f(x), …)\ (x \approx x_0)$.
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: There exists > 0 such that P(f(x), …) holds for all x I with 0 < |x - x_0| < . Written P(f(x), …)\ (x x_0).
Subsets Real Line.hint:
The $0 <$ excludes $x_0$ itself — the property need
  only hold on the punctured neighbourhood $\dot{B}_\delta(x_0)$.
  The limit definition is the special case $P \equiv
  |f(x) - y_0| < \varepsilon$.

Subsets Real Line.label: thm:heine-borel
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex: This is the Theorem: Heine--Borel Theorem for ℝ
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : Heine--Borel Theorem for ℝ

Subsets Real Line.label: thm:heine-borel
Subsets Real Line.question_latex: Sketch the proof of: Heine--Borel Theorem for $\mathbb{R}$.
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: Heine--Borel Theorem for ℝ.
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex: $(a)\Rightarrow(b)$: BW gives convergent subseq; closure lands limit in $X$. $(b)\Rightarrow(a)$: not-closed gives seq escaping $X$, subseq limit must be in $X$ (contradiction). Not-bounded gives $|a_n|>n$, subseq converges hence bounded (contradiction).
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: (a)⇒(b): BW gives convergent subseq; closure lands limit in X. (b)⇒(a): not-closed gives seq escaping X, subseq limit must be in X (contradiction). Not-bounded gives |a_n|>n, subseq converges hence bounded (contradiction).

Subsets Real Line.label: thm:heine-borel
Subsets Real Line.question_latex: What does the proof of Heine--Borel Theorem for $\mathbb{R}$ use?
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: What does the proof of Heine--Borel Theorem for ℝ use?
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex: def:closed-set-R, def:bounded-set-R, thm:bolzano-weierstrass, cor:closed-iff-seq-limits, prop:convergent-bounded, thm:subseq-inherit-limits
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: def:closed-set-R, def:bounded-set-R, thm:bolzano-weierstrass, cor:closed-iff-seq-limits, prop:convergent-bounded, thm:subseq-inherit-limits

Subsets Real Line.label: thm:cluster-point-sequential
Subsets Real Line.question_latex:
What is an adherent point (closure point) of
  $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, and how does it differ from a cluster
  point?
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: What is an adherent point (closure point) of X ⊆ ℝ, and how does it differ from a cluster point?
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex:
An adherent point $x$ satisfies: every neighbourhood of $x$
  meets $X$ (witness $y = x$ permitted).
  A cluster point requires a \emph{distinct} witness $y \neq x$.
  Every cluster point is adherent; an isolated point is adherent
  but not a cluster point.
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: An adherent point x satisfies: every neighbourhood of x meets X (witness y = x permitted). A cluster point requires a distinct witness y ≠ x. Every cluster point is adherent; an isolated point is adherent but not a cluster point.
Subsets Real Line.hint:
Adherent $=$ cluster $\cup$ isolated.
  The closure $\overline{X}$ is the set of all adherent points.

Subsets Real Line.label: thm:cluster-point-sequential
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: \[ c is a cluster point of A \;iff\; (a_n)_{n=1}^∞ ⊆ A \{c\},\; _{n → ∞} a_n = c. \]
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex: This is the Theorem: Sequential Characterization of Cluster Points
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : Sequential Characterization of Cluster Points

Subsets Real Line.label: thm:cluster-point-sequential
Subsets Real Line.question_latex: Give a failure case or negation for: Sequential Characterization of Cluster Points.
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: Give a failure case or negation for: Sequential Characterization of Cluster Points.
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex:
$\{2\}$ is adherent to $\{2\}$ (take $y = x = 2$) but
  $2$ is not a cluster point of $\{2\}$: no distinct witness
  exists.
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: \{2\} is adherent to \{2\} (take y = x = 2) but 2 is not a cluster point of \{2\}: no distinct witness exists.

Subsets Real Line.label: thm:cluster-point-sequential
Subsets Real Line.question_latex: What does Sequential Characterization of Cluster Points immediately give you?
Subsets Real Line.question_plain: What does Sequential Characterization of Cluster Points immediately give you?
Subsets Real Line.answer_latex: def:closure-R
Subsets Real Line.answer_plain: def:closure-R

Topology · Point Set Topology.label: thm:baire-preview
Topology · Point Set Topology.question_latex: State the theorem: Baire Category Theorem.
Topology · Point Set Topology.question_plain: State the theorem: Baire Category Theorem.
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_latex:
Let $(X, d)$ be a complete metric space.
Then $X$ is not meager in itself: $X$ cannot be written as a countable
union of nowhere-dense sets.

Equivalently, if $X = \bigcup_{n=1}^\infty E_n$ then at least one $E_n$
has non-empty interior.
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_plain: Let (X, d) be a complete metric space. Then X is not meager in itself: X cannot be written as a countable union of nowhere-dense sets. Equivalently, if X = _{n=1}^∞ E_n then at least one E_n has non-empty interior.

Topology · Point Set Topology.label: cor:classical-heine-borel
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_latex:
A subset $K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is compact if and only if
$K$ is closed and bounded.
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_plain: A subset K ⊆ ℝ^n is compact if and only if K is closed and bounded.

Topology · Point Set Topology.label: prop:nowhere-dense-equiv
Topology · Point Set Topology.question_latex: State the proposition: Complement characterization of nowhere-dense.
Topology · Point Set Topology.question_plain: State the proposition: Complement characterization of nowhere-dense.
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_latex:
$E \subseteq X$ is nowhere-dense if and only if $X \setminus \overline{E}$
is dense in $X$.
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_plain: E ⊆ X is nowhere-dense if and only if X E is dense in X.

Topology · Point Set Topology.label: prop:nowhere-dense-equiv
Topology · Point Set Topology.question_latex: Sketch the proof of: Complement characterization of nowhere-dense.
Topology · Point Set Topology.question_plain: Sketch the proof of: Complement characterization of nowhere-dense.
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_latex: Both conditions say the same thing about open sets: translate ``empty interior of $\overline{E}$” into ``every open set meets the complement,” which is the density criterion.
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_plain: Both conditions say the same thing about open sets: translate ``empty interior of E” into ``every open set meets the complement,” which is the density criterion.

Topology · Point Set Topology.label: prop:nowhere-dense-equiv
Topology · Point Set Topology.question_latex: What does the proof of Complement characterization of nowhere-dense use?
Topology · Point Set Topology.question_plain: What does the proof of Complement characterization of nowhere-dense use?
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_latex:
• Definition~nowhere dense: $\operatorname{int}(\overline{E}) = \varnothing$.
  • Proposition~dense equiv(iii): dense iff meets every open set.
  • Definition of interior: largest open subset of $\overline{E}$.
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_plain: Definition~nowhere dense: int(E) = . Proposition~dense equiv(iii): dense iff meets every open set. Definition of interior: largest open subset of E.

Topology · Point Set Topology.label: thm:gen-heine-borel
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_latex:
Let $(X,d)$ be a complete metric space and $A \subseteq X$.
Then $A$ is compact if and only if $A$ is closed and totally bounded.
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_plain: Let (X,d) be a complete metric space and A ⊆ X. Then A is compact if and only if A is closed and totally bounded.

Topology · Point Set Topology.label: thm:gen-heine-borel
Topology · Point Set Topology.question_plain: \[ (X,d)\ complete metric space,\; A ⊆ X: A\ compact iff (A\ closed ) ∧ ( \,ε>0,\; \, n ℕ,\; \, x_1,…,x_n X,\; A ⊆ _{k=1}^n B_ε(x_k) ). \]
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : Generalized Heine-Borel

Topology · Point Set Topology.label: def:meager
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_latex:
A subset $A \subseteq X$ is \emph{meager} (or \emph{of first category})
if it can be written as a countable union of nowhere-dense sets:
\[
A = \bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} E_n,
\quad \text{where each } E_n \text{ is nowhere-dense.}
\]
A set that is not meager is called \emph{non-meager} (or
\emph{of second category}).
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_plain: A subset A ⊆ X is meager (or of first category) if it can be written as a countable union of nowhere-dense sets: \[ A = _{n=1}^{∞} E_n, where each E_n is nowhere-dense. \] A set that is not meager is called non-meager (or of second category).

Topology · Point Set Topology.label: def:nowhere-dense
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_latex:
Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space and $E \subseteq X$.
We say $E$ is \emph{nowhere-dense in $X$} if the interior of its closure
is empty:
\[
\operatorname{int}(\overline{E}) = \varnothing.
\]
Topology · Point Set Topology.answer_plain: Let (X,d) be a metric space and E ⊆ X. We say E is nowhere-dense in X if the interior of its closure is empty: \[ int(E) = . \]

Topology · Point Set Topology.label: def:nowhere-dense
Topology · Point Set Topology.question_plain: \[ E is nowhere-dense \;iff\; \, x E,\; \, r > 0,\; B_r(x) ⊆ E. \] No point of E has an open ball entirely inside E.

Totally Bounded.label: prop:totally-bounded-Rn
Totally Bounded.name_latex: Bounded and totally bounded coincide in $\mathbb{R}^n$
Totally Bounded.name_plain: Bounded and totally bounded coincide in ℝ^n
Totally Bounded.question_latex: State the proposition: Bounded and totally bounded coincide in $\mathbb{R}^n$.
Totally Bounded.question_plain: State the proposition: Bounded and totally bounded coincide in ℝ^n.
Totally Bounded.answer_latex:
A subset $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is totally bounded if and only if
it is bounded.
Totally Bounded.answer_plain: A subset A ⊆ ℝ^n is totally bounded if and only if it is bounded.

Totally Bounded.label: prop:bounded-not-totally-bounded
Totally Bounded.name_latex: Bounded does not imply totally bounded
Totally Bounded.name_plain: Bounded does not imply totally bounded
Totally Bounded.question_latex: State the proposition: Bounded does not imply totally bounded.
Totally Bounded.question_plain: State the proposition: Bounded does not imply totally bounded.
Totally Bounded.answer_latex: An infinite set with the discrete metric is bounded but not totally bounded.
Totally Bounded.answer_plain: An infinite set with the discrete metric is bounded but not totally bounded.

Totally Bounded.label: def:totally-bounded
Totally Bounded.answer_latex:
Let $(X, d)$ be a metric space and $A \subseteq X$.
We say $A$ is \emph{totally bounded} if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there
exist finitely many points $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in X$ such that
\[
A \;\subseteq\; \bigcup_{k=1}^n B_\varepsilon(x_k).
\]
The finite collection $\{x_1, \ldots, x_n\}$ is called an
\emph{$\varepsilon$-net} for $A$.
Totally Bounded.answer_plain: Let (X, d) be a metric space and A ⊆ X. We say A is totally bounded if for every ε > 0 there exist finitely many points x_1, , x_n X such that \[ A \;⊆\; _{k=1}^n B_ε(x_k). \] The finite collection \{x_1, , x_n\} is called an ε-net for A.

Totally Bounded.label: def:totally-bounded
Totally Bounded.question_plain: \[ A totally bounded \;iff\; \,ε > 0,\; \, n ℕ,\; \, x_1, …, x_n X, A ⊆ _{k=1}^n B_ε(x_k). \]
Totally Bounded.answer_plain: This is the Definition : Totally Bounded

Totally Bounded.label: prop:totally-bounded-implies-bounded
Totally Bounded.question_latex: State the proposition: Totally bounded implies bounded.
Totally Bounded.question_plain: State the proposition: Totally bounded implies bounded.
Totally Bounded.answer_latex: If $A \subseteq X$ is totally bounded, then $A$ is bounded.
Totally Bounded.answer_plain: If A ⊆ X is totally bounded, then A is bounded.

Totally Bounded.label: prop:totally-bounded-Cauchy
Totally Bounded.name_latex: Totally bounded sets have Cauchy subsequences
Totally Bounded.name_plain: Totally bounded sets have Cauchy subsequences
Totally Bounded.question_latex: State the proposition: Totally bounded sets have Cauchy subsequences.
Totally Bounded.question_plain: State the proposition: Totally bounded sets have Cauchy subsequences.
Totally Bounded.answer_latex:
If $A \subseteq X$ is totally bounded, then every sequence in $A$ has
a Cauchy subsequence.
Totally Bounded.answer_plain: If A ⊆ X is totally bounded, then every sequence in A has a Cauchy subsequence.

Trigonometric.label: thm:fundamental-trig-limit
Trigonometric.question_latex: State the fundamental trigonometric limit and its proof method.
Trigonometric.question_plain: State the fundamental trigonometric limit and its proof method.
Trigonometric.answer_latex:
$\lim_{x\to 0}\sin x/x = 1$.
Proof: geometric squeeze $\cos x \leq \sin x / x \leq 1$, then
$\cos x \to 1$.
Trigonometric.answer_plain: _{x→ 0} x/x = 1. Proof: geometric squeeze x ≤ x / x ≤ 1, then x → 1.
Trigonometric.hint:
$\sin x \approx x$ near $0$. This is the first-order Taylor
approximation.

Trigonometric.label: thm:fundamental-trig-limit
Trigonometric.question_latex:
$\forall \varepsilon > 0\;\exists \delta > 0:\;
0 < |x| < \delta \Rightarrow |\sin x / x - 1| < \varepsilon.$
Trigonometric.answer_plain: This is the Theorem : Fundamental Trigonometric Limit

Trigonometric.label: thm:fundamental-trig-limit
Trigonometric.question_latex: Give a failure case or negation for: Fundamental Trigonometric Limit.
Trigonometric.question_plain: Give a failure case or negation for: Fundamental Trigonometric Limit.
Trigonometric.answer_latex:
$\lim_{x\to 0}\sin(ax)/x = a$, not $1$ — the limit of
$\sin(ax)/(ax) = 1$ but $\sin(ax)/x = a \cdot \sin(ax)/(ax) \to a$.
Trigonometric.answer_plain: _{x→ 0} (ax)/x = a, not 1 — the limit of (ax)/(ax) = 1 but (ax)/x = a (ax)/(ax) → a.

Trigonometric.question_latex: What does Fundamental Trigonometric Limit immediately give you?

Trigonometric.label: prop:pythagorean-identity
Trigonometric.question_latex: State and sketch-prove the Pythagorean identity.

Trigonometric.answer_latex:
$\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1$. Proof: the derivative is $0$ and
the value at $0$ is $1$, so it is constantly $1$.

$\forall x \in \mathbb{R}:\; \sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1.$

Trigonometric.label: def:sine-cosine
Trigonometric.name_latex: Sine and Cosine via Power Series
Trigonometric.name_plain: Sine and Cosine via Power Series
Trigonometric.question_latex: Define $\sin x$ and $\cos x$.
Trigonometric.question_plain: Define x and x.
Trigonometric.answer_plain: x = _{n=0}^∞ (-1)^n x^{2n+1}/(2n+1)!, \; x = _{n=0}^∞ (-1)^n x^{2n}/(2n)!. Both converge absolutely for all x ℝ.
Trigonometric.hint:
$\sin$ is the odd part and $\cos$ the even part of the
complex exponential $e^{ix}$.

Trigonometric.label: def:sine-cosine
Trigonometric.name_latex: Sine and Cosine via Power Series
Trigonometric.name_plain: Sine and Cosine via Power Series
Trigonometric.question_latex:
$\forall x \in \mathbb{R}$: both series converge absolutely by the
ratio test since $(2n+1)!$ grows faster than any fixed power.
Trigonometric.question_plain: x ℝ: both series converge absolutely by the ratio test since (2n+1)! grows faster than any fixed power.
Trigonometric.answer_latex: This is the Definition: Sine and Cosine via Power Series
Trigonometric.answer_plain: This is the Definition : Sine and Cosine via Power Series

Trigonometric.label: def:sine-cosine
Trigonometric.name_latex: Sine and Cosine via Power Series
Trigonometric.name_plain: Sine and Cosine via Power Series
Trigonometric.question_latex: Give a failure case or negation for: Sine and Cosine via Power Series.
Trigonometric.question_plain: Give a failure case or negation for: Sine and Cosine via Power Series.
Trigonometric.answer_latex:
The series definitions are exact. The ``unit circle'' picture
is a geometric consequence, not the definition.
Trigonometric.answer_plain: The series definitions are exact. The ``unit circle'' picture is a geometric consequence, not the definition.

Trigonometric.label: def:other-trig
Trigonometric.question_latex: Define $\tan x$ and state its Pythagorean identity.
Trigonometric.question_plain: Define x and state its Pythagorean identity.
Trigonometric.answer_latex: $\tan x = \sin x / \cos x$. Identity: $1 + \tan^2 x = \sec^2 x$.
Trigonometric.answer_plain: x = x / x. Identity: 1 + ^2 x = ^2 x.
Trigonometric.hint: Divide $\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1$ through by $\cos^2 x$.

% TODO

$\forall x \in \mathbb{R}$ with $\sin x \neq 0$: $\cot x = \cos x/\sin x$ and $\csc x = 1/\sin x$.
Trigonometric.question_plain: % TODO x ℝ with x ≠ 0: x = x/ x and x = 1/ x. x ℝ with x ≠ 0: x = x/ x and x = 1/ x.
Trigonometric.answer_latex: This is the Definition: Tangent, Cotangent, Secant, Cosecant
